Cross-datacenter replication between database clusters happens asynchronously in the background, in a style that is similar to multi-leader replication [52]. Detecting Concurrent Writes Dynamo-style databases allow several clients to concurrently write to the same key, which means that conflicts will occur even if strict quorums are used. The situation is similar to multi-leader replication (see “Handling Write Conflicts” on page 171), although in Dynamo-style databases conflicts can also arise during read repair or hinted handoff. 184 | Chapter 5: Replication The problem is that events may arrive in a different order at different nodes, due to variable network delays and partial failures. For example, Figure 5-12 shows two cli‐ ents, A and B, simultaneously writing to a key X in a three-node datastore: • Node 1 receives the write from A, but never receives the write from B due to a transient outage. • Node 2 first receives the write from A, then the write from B. • Node 3 first receives the write from B, then the write from A. Figure 5-12. Concurrent writes in a Dynamo-style datastore: there is no well-defined ordering. If each node simply overwrote the value for a key whenever it received a write request from a client, the nodes would become permanently inconsistent, as shown by the final get request in Figure 5-12: node 2 thinks that the final value of X is B, whereas the other nodes think that the value is A. In order to become eventually consistent, the replicas should converge toward the same value. How do they do that? One might hope that replicated databases would handle this automatically, but unfortunately most implementations are quite poor: if you want to avoid losing data, you—the application developer—need to know a lot about the internals of your database’s conflict handling. We briefly touched on some techniques for conflict resolution in “Handling Write Conflicts” on page 171. Before we wrap up this chapter, let’s explore the issue in a bit more detail. Leaderless Replication | 185 Last write wins (discarding concurrent writes) One approach for achieving eventual convergence is to declare that each replica need only store the most “recent” value and allow “older” values to be overwritten and dis‐ carded. Then, as long as we have some way of unambiguously determining which write is more “recent,” and every write is eventually copied to every replica, the repli‐ cas will eventually converge to the same value. As indicated by the quotes around “recent,” this idea is actually quite misleading. In the example of Figure 5-12, neither client knew about the other one when it sent its write requests to the database nodes, so it’s not clear which one happened first. In fact, it doesn’t really make sense to say that either happened “first”: we say the writes are concurrent, so their order is undefined. Even though the writes don’t have a natural ordering, we can force an arbitrary order on them. For example, we can attach a timestamp to each write, pick the biggest timestamp as the most “recent,” and discard any writes with an earlier timestamp. This conflict resolution algorithm, called last write wins (LWW), is the only sup‐ ported conflict resolution method in Cassandra [53], and an optional feature in Riak [35]. LWW achieves the goal of eventual convergence, but at the cost of durability: if there are several concurrent writes to the same key, even if they were all reported as suc‐ cessful to the client (because they were written to w replicas), only one of the writes will survive and the others will be silently discarded. Moreover, LWW may even drop writes that are not concurrent, as we shall discuss in “Timestamps for ordering events” on page 291. There are some situations, such as caching, in which lost writes are perhaps accepta‐ ble. If losing data is not acceptable, LWW is a poor choice for conflict resolution. The only safe way of using a database with LWW is to ensure that a key is only writ‐ ten once and thereafter treated as immutable, thus avoiding any concurrent updates to the same key. For example, a recommended way of using Cassandra is to use a UUID as the key, thus giving each write operation a unique key [53]. The “happens-before” relationship and concurrency How do we decide whether two operations are concurrent or not? To develop an intuition, let’s look at some examples: • In Figure 5-9, the two writes are not concurrent: A’s insert happens before B’s increment, because the value incremented by B is the value inserted by A. In other words, B’s operation builds upon A’s operation, so B’s operation must have happened later. We also say that B is causally dependent on A. 186 | Chapter 5: Replication • On the other hand, the two writes in Figure 5-12 are concurrent: when each cli‐ ent starts the operation, it does not know that another client is also performing an operation on the same key. Thus, there is no causal dependency between the operations. An operation A happens before another operation B if B knows about A, or depends on A, or builds upon A in some way. Whether one operation happens before another operation is the key to defining what concurrency means. In fact, we can simply say that two operations are concurrent if neither happens before the other (i.e., neither knows about the other) [54]. Thus, whenever you have two operations A and B, there are three possibilities: either A happened before B, or B happened before A, or A and B are concurrent. What we need is an algorithm to tell us whether two operations are concurrent or not. If one operation happened before another, the later operation should overwrite the earlier operation, but if the operations are concurrent, we have a conflict that needs to be resolved. Concurrency, Time, and Relativity It may seem that two operations should be called concurrent if they occur “at the same time”—but in fact, it is not important whether they literally overlap in time. Because of problems with clocks in distributed systems, it is actually quite difficult to tell whether two things happened at exactly the same time—an issue we will discuss in more detail in Chapter 8. For defining concurrency, exact time doesn’t matter: we simply call two operations concurrent if they are both unaware of each other, regardless of the physical time at which they occurred. People sometimes make a connection between this principle and the special theory of relativity in physics [54], which introduced the idea that information cannot travel faster than the speed of light. Consequently, two events that occur some distance apart cannot possibly affect each other if the time between the events is shorter than the time it takes light to travel the distance between them. In computer systems, two operations might be concurrent even though the speed of light would in principle have allowed one operation to affect the other. For example, if the network was slow or interrupted at the time, two operations can occur some time apart and still be concurrent, because the network problems prevented one operation from being able to know about the other. Capturing the happens-before relationship Let’s look at an algorithm that determines whether two operations are concurrent, or whether one happened before another. To keep things simple, let’s start with a data‐ Leaderless Replication | 187 base that has only one replica. Once we have worked out how to do this on a single replica, we can generalize the approach to a leaderless database with multiple replicas. Figure 5-13 shows two clients concurrently adding items to the same shopping cart. (If that example strikes you as too inane, imagine instead two air traffic controllers concurrently adding aircraft to the sector they are tracking.) Initially, the cart is empty. Between them, the clients make five writes to the database: 1. Client 1 adds milk to the cart. This is the first write to that key, so the server suc‐ cessfully stores it and assigns it version 1. The server also echoes the value back to the client, along with the version number. 2. Client 2 adds eggs to the cart, not knowing that client 1 concurrently added milk (client 2 thought that its eggs were the only item in the cart). The server assigns version 2 to this write, and stores eggs and milk as two separate values. It then returns both values to the client, along with the version number of 2. 3. Client 1, oblivious to client 2’s write, wants to add flour to the cart, so it thinks the current cart contents should be [milk, flour]. It sends this value to the server, along with the version number 1 that the server gave client 1 previously. The server can tell from the version number that the write of [milk, flour] supersedes the prior value of [milk] but that it is concurrent with [eggs]. Thus, the server assigns version 3 to [milk, flour], overwrites the version 1 value [milk], but keeps the version 2 value [eggs] and returns both remaining values to the client. 4. Meanwhile, client 2 wants to add ham to the cart, unaware that client 1 just added flour. Client 2 received the two values [milk] and [eggs] from the server in the last response, so the client now merges those values and adds ham to form a new value, [eggs, milk, ham]. It sends that value to the server, along with the previ‐ ous version number 2. The server detects that version 2 overwrites [eggs] but is concurrent with [milk, flour], so the two remaining values are [milk, flour] with version 3, and [eggs, milk, ham] with version 4. 5. Finally, client 1 wants to add bacon. It previously received [milk, flour] and [eggs] from the server at version 3, so it merges those, adds bacon, and sends the final value [milk, flour, eggs, bacon] to the server, along with the version number 3. This overwrites [milk, flour] (note that [eggs] was already over‐ written in the last step) but is concurrent with [eggs, milk, ham], so the server keeps those two concurrent values. 188 | Chapter 5: Replication Figure 5-13. Capturing causal dependencies between two clients concurrently editing a shopping cart. The dataflow between the operations in Figure 5-13 is illustrated graphically in Figure 5-14. The arrows indicate which operation happened before which other oper‐ ation, in the sense that the later operation knew about or depended on the earlier one. In this example, the clients are never fully up to date with the data on the server, since there is always another operation going on concurrently. But old versions of the value do get overwritten eventually, and no writes are lost. Figure 5-14. Graph of causal dependencies in Figure 5-13. Note that the server can determine whether two operations are concurrent by looking at the version numbers—it does not need to interpret the value itself (so the value could be any data structure). The algorithm works as follows: Leaderless Replication | 189 • The server maintains a version number for every key, increments the version number every time that key is written, and stores the new version number along with the value written. • When a client reads a key, the server returns all values that have not been over‐ written, as well as the latest version number. A client must read a key before writing. • When a client writes a key, it must include the version number from the prior read, and it must merge together all values that it received in the prior read. (The response from a write request can be like a read, returning all current values, which allows us to chain several writes like in the shopping cart example.) • When the server receives a write with a particular version number, it can over‐ write all values with that version number or below (since it knows that they have been merged into the new value), but it must keep all values with a higher ver‐ sion number (because those values are concurrent with the incoming write). When a write includes the version number from a prior read, that tells us which pre‐ vious state the write is based on. If you make a write without including a version number, it is concurrent with all other writes, so it will not overwrite anything—it will just be returned as one of the values on subsequent reads. Merging concurrently written values This algorithm ensures that no data is silently dropped, but it unfortunately requires that the clients do some extra work: if several operations happen concurrently, clients have to clean up afterward by merging the concurrently written values. Riak calls these concurrent values siblings. Merging sibling values is essentially the same problem as conflict resolution in multileader replication, which we discussed previously (see “Handling Write Conflicts” on page 171). A simple approach is to just pick one of the values based on a version number or timestamp (last write wins), but that implies losing data. So, you may need to do something more intelligent in application code. With the example of a shopping cart, a reasonable approach to merging siblings is to just take the union. In Figure 5-14, the two final siblings are [milk, flour, eggs, bacon] and [eggs, milk, ham]; note that milk and eggs appear in both, even though they were each only written once. The merged value might be something like [milk, flour, eggs, bacon, ham], without duplicates. However, if you want to allow people to also remove things from their carts, and not just add things, then taking the union of siblings may not yield the right result: if you merge two sibling carts and an item has been removed in only one of them, then the removed item will reappear in the union of the siblings [37]. To prevent this prob‐ 190 | Chapter 5: Replication lem, an item cannot simply be deleted from the database when it is removed; instead, the system must leave a marker with an appropriate version number to indicate that the item has been removed when merging siblings. Such a deletion marker is known as a tombstone. (We previously saw tombstones in the context of log compaction in “Hash Indexes” on page 72.) As merging siblings in application code is complex and error-prone, there are some efforts to design data structures that can perform this merging automatically, as dis‐ cussed in “Automatic Conflict Resolution” on page 174. For example, Riak’s datatype support uses a family of data structures called CRDTs [38, 39, 55] that can automati‐ cally merge siblings in sensible ways, including preserving deletions. Version vectors The example in Figure 5-13 used only a single replica. How does the algorithm change when there are multiple replicas, but no leader? Figure 5-13 uses a single version number to capture dependencies between opera‐ tions, but that is not sufficient when there are multiple replicas accepting writes con‐ currently. Instead, we need to use a version number per replica as well as per key. Each replica increments its own version number when processing a write, and also keeps track of the version numbers it has seen from each of the other replicas. This information indicates which values to overwrite and which values to keep as siblings. The collection of version numbers from all the replicas is called a version vector [56]. A few variants of this idea are in use, but the most interesting is probably the dotted version vector [57], which is used in Riak 2.0 [58, 59]. We won’t go into the details, but the way it works is quite similar to what we saw in our cart example. Like the version numbers in Figure 5-13, version vectors are sent from the database replicas to clients when values are read, and need to be sent back to the database when a value is subsequently written. (Riak encodes the version vector as a string that it calls causal context.) The version vector allows the database to distinguish between overwrites and concurrent writes. Also, like in the single-replica example, the application may need to merge siblings. The version vector structure ensures that it is safe to read from one replica and subse‐ quently write back to another replica. Doing so may result in siblings being created, but no data is lost as long as siblings are merged correctly. Version vectors and vector clocks A version vector is sometimes also called a vector clock, even though they are not quite the same. The difference is subtle—please see the references for details [57, 60, 61]. In brief, when comparing the state of replicas, version vectors are the right data structure to use. Leaderless Replication | 191 Summary In this chapter we looked at the issue of replication. Replication can serve several purposes: High availability Keeping the system running, even when one machine (or several machines, or an entire datacenter) goes down Disconnected operation Allowing an application to continue working when there is a network interrup‐ tion Latency Placing data geographically close to users, so that users can interact with it faster Scalability Being able to handle a higher volume of reads than a single machine could han‐ dle, by performing reads on replicas Despite being a simple goal—keeping a copy of the same data on several machines— replication turns out to be a remarkably tricky problem. It requires carefully thinking about concurrency and about all the things that can go wrong, and dealing with the consequences of those faults. At a minimum, we need to deal with unavailable nodes and network interruptions (and that’s not even considering the more insidious kinds of fault, such as silent data corruption due to software bugs). We discussed three main approaches to replication: Single-leader replication Clients send all writes to a single node (the leader), which sends a stream of data change events to the other replicas (followers). Reads can be performed on any replica, but reads from followers might be stale. Multi-leader replication Clients send each write to one of several leader nodes, any of which can accept writes. The leaders send streams of data change events to each other and to any follower nodes. Leaderless replication Clients send each write to several nodes, and read from several nodes in parallel in order to detect and correct nodes with stale data. Each approach has advantages and disadvantages. Single-leader replication is popular because it is fairly easy to understand and there is no conflict resolution to worry about. Multi-leader and leaderless replication can be more robust in the presence of 192 | Chapter 5: Replication faulty nodes, network interruptions, and latency spikes—at the cost of being harder to reason about and providing only very weak consistency guarantees. Replication can be synchronous or asynchronous, which has a profound effect on the system behavior when there is a fault. Although asynchronous replication can be fast when the system is running smoothly, it’s important to figure out what happens when replication lag increases and servers fail. If a leader fails and you promote an asynchronously updated follower to be the new leader, recently committed data may be lost. We looked at some strange effects that can be caused by replication lag, and we dis‐ cussed a few consistency models which are helpful for deciding how an application should behave under replication lag: Read-after-write consistency Users should always see data that they submitted themselves. Monotonic reads After users have seen the data at one point in time, they shouldn’t later see the data from some earlier point in time. Consistent prefix reads Users should see the data in a state that makes causal sense: for example, seeing a question and its reply in the correct order. Finally, we discussed the concurrency issues that are inherent in multi-leader and leaderless replication approaches: because they allow multiple writes to happen con‐ currently, conflicts may occur. We examined an algorithm that a database might use to determine whether one operation happened before another, or whether they hap‐ pened concurrently. We also touched on methods for resolving conflicts by merging together concurrent updates. In the next chapter we will continue looking at data that is distributed across multiple machines, through the counterpart of replication: splitting a large dataset into parti‐ tions. instruction: However, partitioning is the most established term, so we’ll stick with that. Normally, partitions are defined in such a way that each piece of data (each record, row, or document) belongs to exactly one partition.
output:
The most common way to partition a dataset is by a column (or set of columns) that exhibits a relatively uniform distribution of data.

For example, if we have a large table of orders, and we want to partition it to improve query performance, a good choice might be to partition by order date. This way, orders from a particular month or year will all be stored together in one partition.

When a query is executed, the database can then use the partitioning scheme to determine which partition(s) to access, reducing the overall amount of data that needs to be searched.

instruction: Some systems are designed for transactional work‐ loads, and others for analytics (see “Transaction Processing or Analytics?” on page 90): this difference affects how the system is tuned, but the fundamentals of partition‐ ing apply to both kinds of workloads.
output:
Transaction processing systems process requests one at a time, with each request being completed before the next one starts. This means that a transaction processing system can guarantee that no request will be lost or processed out of order.

Analytics systems, on the other hand, process large amounts of data, often in parallel, and don't require the same level of order or completeness as transaction processing systems. Analytics systems can return results as soon as they are available, without waiting for every last piece of data to be processed.

instruction: In this chapter we will first look at different approaches for partitioning large datasets and observe how the indexing of data interacts with partitioning. We’ll then talk about rebalancing, which is necessary if you want to add or remove nodes in your cluster.
output:
We will start with a discussion on range-based partitioning. Range-based partitioning divides a dataset into partitions based on a range of values. This can be useful when dealing with datasets that can be naturally divided along a specific range.

We will then talk about hash-based partitioning. Hash-based partitioning divides a dataset into partitions based on the hash value of a key. This can be useful when dealing with datasets that do not have a natural ordering or partition key.

We will finish this chapter by talking about the importance of rebalancing and how it can be done efficiently. Rebalancing is necessary when you add or remove nodes in a cluster to ensure that data is evenly distributed. It is important to note that rebalancing can be time-consuming and resource-intensive, so it is best to minimize the number of nodes added or removed to avoid lengthy rebalancing processes.

instruction: Finally, we’ll get an overview of how databases route requests to the right par‐ titions and execute queries. Partitioning and Replication Partitioning is usually combined with replication so that copies of each partition are stored on multiple nodes.
output:
A partition is a logical division of a table into smaller, more manageable chunks. The partitioning type determines which column(s) are used to partition the table. For example, a table with a column for year of birth can be partitioned by year, so that all records with a year of birth between 1960 and 1969 are stored in one partition, while records from 1970 to 1979 are stored in another.

The database routing algorithm selects the node that currently owns the partition containing the requested data. When a query is executed, the database queries the metadata to find the partition that matches the query criteria, and it sends the query to that node.

Replication is the process of making multiple copies of data. In a master-slave replication setup, one node acts as the master, and all other nodes act as slaves. The master sends changes to the data to the slaves, and the slaves apply the changes. This allows the database to distribute the workload and improve query performance.

instruction: Combining replication and partitioning: each node acts as leader for some partitions and follower for other partitions. Partitioning of Key-Value Data Say you have a large amount of data, and you want to partition it.
output:
First, choose a partitioning key. A partitioning key is a column used to determine how to distribute rows across multiple nodes. You can partition by any column, but it is most common to partition by a column that has a high degree of uniformity and a low degree of redundancy.

For example, if you have a large table of users, you might choose the user_id column as the partitioning key, since each user typically has a unique id. The database would then distribute the rows across the nodes based on the user_id values.

Now, let's talk about combining replication and partitioning. Replication is used to make data consistent across multiple nodes. Partitioning is used to distribute data across multiple nodes. When you combine these two techniques, each node acts as a leader for some partitions and a follower for other partitions.

The leader node is responsible for handling read and write requests for its partitions. The follower nodes maintain a copy of the data from the leader node and also participate in consensus protocols to ensure data consistency. This approach provides both scalability and availability, as each node can handle a certain amount of read and write requests, and if one node goes

instruction: How do you decide which records to store on which nodes? Our goal with partitioning is to spread the data and the query load evenly across nodes. If every node takes a fair share, then—in theory—10 nodes should be able to handle 10 times as much data and 10 times the read and write throughput of a single node (ignoring replication for now).
output:
But what is a fair share, and how do you measure it? The simplest way is to divide the data into roughly equal parts, and assign each part to a node. But what if the data isn't evenly distributed? The best way to determine fairness in this context is to use a consistent hashing algorithm.

Consistent hashing is a method for distributing keys to nodes in a hash ring. Each node is assigned a virtual position around the ring, and each key is hashed to a position on the ring. The key is then assigned to the node closest to its position. This ensures that the load is spread evenly across nodes.

So, how does consistent hashing distribute keys to nodes in a hash ring? The algorithm works by first creating a large virtual ring, and assigning each node a unique position on the ring. Then, it applies a hash function to the keys, and assigns each key to the node whose position is closest to the hash value. This process ensures that keys are distributed evenly across nodes.

What are the main steps to implement consistent hashing in a distributed database?
1. Create a virtual ring with each node assigned a unique position
2. Apply a hash function

instruction: If the partitioning is unfair, so that some partitions have more data or queries than others, we call it skewed. The presence of skew makes partitioning much less effective. In an extreme case, all the load could end up on one partition, so 9 out of 10 nodes are idle and your bottleneck is the single busy node.
output:

instruction: A partition with disproportion‐ ately high load is called a hot spot. The simplest approach for avoiding hot spots would be to assign records to nodes randomly. That would distribute the data quite evenly across the nodes, but it has a big disadvantage: when you’re trying to read a particular item, you have no way of knowing which node it is on, so you have to query all nodes in parallel.
output:
This is inefficient because each node will waste resources on unneeded responses. One solution is to use a consistent hashing algorithm. A consistent hashing algorithm maps each key to a node, such that if a key is moved to a new node, only a small fraction of the keys move with it. This is called a localized rehashing.

Another solution is to use a load-balancing algorithm. A load-balancing algorithm attempts to distribute the data as evenly as possible across all nodes. This can be done through techniques such as round-robin, random key-based, or virtual-node placement.

instruction: We can do better. Let’s assume for now that you have a simple key-value data model, in which you always access a record by its primary key. For example, in an oldfashioned paper encyclopedia, you look up an entry by its title; since all the entries are alphabetically sorted by title, you can quickly find the one you’re looking for.
output:
In a database, a primary key uniquely identifies each record. If you want to retrieve a record, you always have to know its primary key.

Suppose you have a large number of records, and you want to find all the records whose value matches a certain query string. In this case, you would have to search the entire database, which could be a time-consuming process.

Now suppose you have an index on some field in the record. An index is a data structure that allows you to search the database much more efficiently. With an index, you can quickly find all the records that have a given value in the indexed field.

For example, if you have an index on the last name field in a database of people, you can quickly find all the records for people with the last name "Smith". This is much faster than searching the entire database.

An index is a useful tool for improving query performance, but it adds complexity to the database. You have to design and maintain the indexes, and they take up extra space in the database.

instruction: If you know the boundaries between the ranges, you can easily deter‐ mine which partition contains a given key. If you also know which partition is assigned to which node, then you can make your request directly to the appropriate node (or, in the case of the encyclopedia, pick the correct book off the shelf).
output:
The trick to efficient partitioning is to keep the number of elements in each partition small. If the range of each partition is too large, then the probability that a given key will fall within that partition is too high. In extreme cases, if all the keys fall within a single partition, then you have no partitioning at all.

To keep the number of elements in each partition small, you can divide the key space into equal-sized partitions. One common method is to use a hash function to map each key to a partition. Hash functions distribute keys evenly across partitions, so this method is very effective for large key spaces.

However, there are cases where the keys do not distribute evenly. For example, if the keys are all integers between 1 and 1000, then most keys will fall into partitions with smaller numbers, and the larger partitions will be relatively empty. In such cases, you may need to adjust the size of the partitions to balance the load.

instruction: The partition boundaries might be chosen manually by an administrator, or the data‐ base can choose them automatically (we will discuss choices of partition boundaries in more detail in “Rebalancing Partitions” on page 209).
output:
Manually chosen partition boundaries may be based on physical or logical criteria. Physical criteria include size, location, or connectivity of the data. Logical criteria include functional or application-specific requirements. For example, an organization might choose to partition its database into two parts based on the product line. This would result in two separate logical partitions, even though the data might be physically located on the same set of disks.

Automatically chosen partition boundaries are determined by the database management system. The system tries to optimize for factors such as load balancing and even distribution of data. The system may also take into account the characteristics of the data, such as its access patterns or growth rates.

instruction: Many programming languages have simple hash functions built in (as they are used for hash tables), but they may not be suitable for partitioning: for example, in Java’s Object.hashCode() and Ruby’s Object#hash, the same key may have a different hash value in different processes [6].
output:
If you want to make a hash function suitable for partitioning, you need to make sure that the same key always generates the same hash value. This is called a consistent hash function.

Consistent hash functions are typically based on a hash ring. A hash ring is a data structure that stores keys in a hash table, where the hash function maps keys to locations in a virtual ring. The ring is divided into zones, and each zone is assigned to a node. When a key is to be stored, its hash value is used to determine its location in the ring. The closest node to that location is chosen to store the key.

instruction: The partition boundaries can be evenly spaced, or they can be chosen pseudorandomly (in which case the technique is sometimes known as consistent hashing). Consistent Hashing Consistent hashing, as defined by Karger et al.
output:
in 1997, is a method for mapping keys to nodes in a distributed hash table. It is a way to partition a key space into fixed-size regions, each of which is associated with a node in the table.

When a new key is added to the table, the hash value of that key is calculated and the node responsible for the bucket with the smallest hash value is chosen to accept the new key. This has the effect of spreading keys evenly across the nodes in the table, which minimizes the amount of data that needs to be moved when nodes are added or removed.

instruction: [7], is a way of evenly distributing load across an internet-wide system of caches such as a content delivery network (CDN). It uses randomly chosen partition boundaries to avoid the need for central control or distributed consensus.
output:
When a CDN receives a request for a resource, it calculates the hash of that resource's URL and uses the hash's value as an index into a hash table to determine which cache should serve the resource.

This method allows the CDN to distribute the load evenly across the network, reducing the chances of congestion or overload at any single cache. It also allows for easy scaling of the CDN by simply adding more caches to the network.

instruction: Because this is so confusing, it’s best to avoid the term consistent hashing and just call it hash partitioning instead. Unfortunately however, by using the hash of the key for partitioning we lose a nice property of key-range partitioning: the ability to do efficient range queries.

For example, to get all the records in the range [10, 20] with key-range partitioning, we can simply request all the partitions whose range includes the key 10 and 20. With hash partitioning, we would have to compute the hash for every single record and check if it falls within the desired range.

That said, hash partitioning does have its advantages over key-range partitioning. For one, it is generally easier to manage and scale. With key-range partitioning, we would have to rebalance the data every time a key is added or removed. With hash partitioning, we can simply add or remove a bucket, which is much easier to do.

instruction: Different users may be stored on different partitions, but within each user, the updates are stored ordered by timestamp on a single partition. Skewed Workloads and Relieving Hot Spots As discussed, hashing a key to determine its partition can help reduce hot spots.

One solution for skewed workloads is to use a range of partitions, rather than a single partition.

In this approach, the data for each user falls within a specific range of partitions, so each user's updates are spread out across multiple partitions.

This not only reduces the likelihood of hot spots, but it also allows for better load balancing, as each partition will receive roughly equal amounts of writes.

instruction: Thus, you also need some way of keeping track of which keys are being split. Perhaps in the future, data systems will be able to automatically detect and compen‐ sate for skewed workloads; but for now, you need to think through the trade-offs for your own application.
output:
One common way to handle split keys is to use a data structure called a **trie**. A trie is a tree-like data structure that is used to store strings. Each node in the trie represents a character. A path through the trie represents a key. If a key is used frequently, a node in the trie that represents one of its prefixes will have many children. This means that the trie will have a shorter average path length for frequently used keys.

When a key is split, each part is inserted into the trie as a separate key. If a node in the trie represents a split key, it will have multiple children—one for each part of the split key.

For example, suppose the keys in a database are the names of cities. The city names are split into two parts: the first part is the name of the country and the second part is the name of the city. If the country "USA" has 50 cities and the city "New York" is split into two parts: "New" and "York," then there will be 51 keys in the trie: one key for each country name and one key for each city name. Additionally, there

instruction: Partitioning of Key-Value Data | 205 Partitioning and Secondary Indexes The partitioning schemes we have discussed so far rely on a key-value data model. If records are only ever accessed via their primary key, we can determine the partition from that key and use it to route read and write requests to the partition responsible for that key.
output:
However, if we want to support secondary indexes, we must consider a different partitioning strategy. Secondary indexes map values to keys, not the other way around, and this can complicate partitioning. We will discuss three common approaches to partitioning key-value data with secondary indexes.

First, we can partition by range. In range partitioning, we split our data into partitions based on a range of keys. For example, we might partition our data so that all keys between 0 and 100 go to one partition, keys between 100 and 200 go to another partition, and so on.

Second, we can partition by hash. In hash partitioning, we use a hash function to determine the partition for each key. The hash function maps each key to a partition based on the hash value, and we distribute the data evenly across partitions.

Third, we can use composite partitioning, which combines the advantages of both range and hash partitioning. In composite partitioning, we split our data into partitions based on both a range of keys and a hash value. For example, we might partition our data into partitions based on a range of keys

instruction: The situation becomes more complicated if secondary indexes are involved (see also “Other Indexing Structures” on page 85). A secondary index usually doesn’t identify a record uniquely but rather is a way of searching for occurrences of a particular value: find all actions by user 123, find all articles containing the word hogwash, find all cars whose color is red, and so on.
output:
A secondary index is a separate data structure that stores the indexed columns (as keys) along with their corresponding record addresses. This is called an indexed scan. Because secondary indexes do not store all the data of a record, an indexed scan is not as fast as a primary key lookup. However, when searching for specific data based on indexed columns, secondary indexes can be more efficient than full table scans.

When a secondary index is added to an already indexed table, it is called a multi-index table. A multi-index table is a table with multiple indexes. A multi-index table can be searched using any of its indexes. This provides flexibility in choosing the most efficient index for a particular query.

What are the advantages of a secondary index over a full table scan?

Why is a multi-index table more flexible than a single indexed table?

What information is stored in a secondary index's data structure?

instruction: Secondary indexes are the bread and butter of relational databases, and they are com‐ mon in document databases too. Many key-value stores (such as HBase and Volde‐ mort) have avoided secondary indexes because of their added implementation complexity, but some (such as Riak) have started adding them because they are so useful for data modeling.
output:
The most common use of secondary indexes is to allow for efficient querying based on a column other than the primary key. For example, in a customer database, a secondary index on a customer’s name could allow for very fast lookups by name, while the primary key might be an ID number.

Secondary indexes work by maintaining a separate data structure (index) for each column that has an index. This index is typically built as a B-tree, and it is kept in memory for fast access. When data is added or removed, the index is updated accordingly, so that queries can continue to use the index for fast lookups.

In summary, secondary indexes allow for fast and efficient querying on columns other than the primary key, making them an important tool for data modeling and querying in both relational and document databases.

instruction: And finally, secondary indexes are the raison d’être of search servers such as Solr and Elasticsearch. The problem with secondary indexes is that they don’t map neatly to partitions. There are two main approaches to partitioning a database with secondary indexes: document-based partitioning and term-based partitioning.
output:
In document-based partitioning, an entire document is assigned to a single partition. This is useful when the documents are large and have many fields or when the relationships between documents are complex.

In term-based partitioning, a term is assigned to a single partition. This is useful when the data is text-heavy and the search queries are based on terms.

instruction: Partitioning Secondary Indexes by Document For example, imagine you are operating a website for selling used cars (illustrated in Figure 6-4). Each listing has a unique ID—call it the document ID—and you partition the database by the document ID (for example, IDs 0 to 499 in partition 0, IDs 500 to 999 in partition 1, etc.).
output:
Suppose a secondary index on make has been created: an index that maps each make (say, "Ford") to the set of document IDs that store cars with that make. When a query requests cars of make "Ford," the database engine partitions the index based on the document partitioning of the data; that is, it consults partition 0 for IDs 0 to 499, partition 1 for IDs 500 to 999, and so on.

This partitioning of the index is done on the server side and is usually transparent to the client, meaning that the client does not need to know the partitioning scheme to issue queries efficiently. However, if you wanted to, you could expose the partitioning information to the client through an API.

instruction: ii. If your database only supports a key-value model, you might be tempted to implement a secondary index yourself by creating a mapping from values to document IDs in application code.
output:
However, this approach can lead to issues with data consistency. Consider a scenario where a document with a particular value, say 'John Smith', is updated. If multiple applications try to access the secondary index at the same time, one application could read the old document and the other the new one, leading to inconsistent results.

Moreover, implementing a secondary index in this manner can also cause performance issues. Each time a document is updated, all applications that maintain the secondary index would need to be notified and update their own copies.

To avoid these issues, it's recommended to use a database that supports secondary indexes natively. With a database that offers this functionality, updating a document does not require applications to manage secondary indexes. Instead, the database can handle this automatically, ensuring data consistency and reducing the need for frequent updates to application code.

instruction: 206 | Chapter 6: Partitioning Figure 6-4. Partitioning secondary indexes by document. In this indexing approach, each partition is completely separate: each partition main‐ tains its own secondary indexes, covering only the documents in that partition.
output:
Primary indexes are partitioned based on range, but secondary indexes can be partitioned differently, such as by document number.

A partitioned secondary index provides several benefits:
1. It improves query performance by reducing the number of documents searched for a given query.
2. It allows parallel queries across the partitions.
3. It provides fault tolerance by distributing the index over multiple servers.

For example, suppose a database contains 10,000 documents. If we partition the database into 10 partitions, each partition will have 1,000 documents on average. When we partition a secondary index by document number, each partition will hold indexes for a subset of the documents: say 250 documents in partition 1, 350 documents in partition 2, and so on. This way, when a user queries for a specific document, say document number 500, the query will be directed only to the partition(s) that hold the documents for that number.

instruction: In an ideal world, the index would always be up to date, and every document written to the database would immediately be reflected in the index. However, in a termpartitioned index, that would require a distributed transaction across all partitions affected by a write, which is not supported in all databases (see Chapter 7 and Chap‐ ter 9).
output:
Instead, termpartitioned indexes are updated in batches using a process called merge-indexing. When a write occurs, the database checks whether the affected index blocks are present in the memory cache. If they are, the database applies the write locally and triggers a merge-indexing process for the affected index blocks. If the affected blocks are not in memory, the write request is queued and the database waits for the blocks to be read back in from disk before processing the write.

Once a batch of writes has accumulated, the merge-indexing process begins. The process first identifies the index blocks that need to be merged, and then it merges the blocks together, applying any necessary updates or deletions. The merged blocks are then written back to disk, and the index is updated accordingly.

instruction: Rebalancing Partitions Over time, things change in a database: • The query throughput increases, so you want to add more CPUs to handle the load. • The dataset size increases, so you want to add more disks and RAM to store it.
output:
To handle these changes, you need to rebalance partitions.

Rebalancing means:

1. Moving data between partitions based on a new hash function.
2. Splitting partitions if they get too large.
3. Merging partitions if they get too small.

Why do we need to rebalance partitions?

1. To make sure that the data is evenly distributed across all partitions.
2. To maintain good performance and query throughput.
3. To efficiently store and manage the dataset size.

How often should we rebalance partitions?

1. It depends on the rate of change in query throughput and dataset size.
2. A good practice is to schedule regular rebalancing as a preventative maintenance measure.
3. Monitor performance metrics to determine when it's necessary to rebalance.

instruction: Rebalancing Partitions | 209 Strategies for Rebalancing There are a few different ways of assigning partitions to nodes [23]. Let’s briefly dis‐ cuss each in turn. How not to do it: hash mod N When partitioning by the hash of a key, we said earlier (Figure 6-3) that it’s best to divide the possible hashes into ranges and assign each range to a partition (e.g., assign key to partition 0 if 0 ≤ hash(key) < b , to partition 1 if b ≤ hash(key) < b , etc.).
output:
However, it’s also possible to use a hash mod N approach, where we take the hash modulo N to get a partition index. For example, if we have 4 partitions (N = 4) and the hash is 1003, we’d assign it to partition 3 (1003 % 4 = 3).

This approach is simple, but it has several drawbacks. The most significant is that it tends to create hot spots: partitions that receive many more keys than others. This can lead to imbalanced workloads and uneven resource utilization.

instruction: 0 0 1 Perhaps you wondered why we don’t just use mod (the % operator in many program‐ ming languages). For example, hash(key) mod 10 would return a number between 0 and 9 (if we write the hash as a decimal number, the hash mod 10 would be the last digit).
output:
1 1 1 This is a good idea in theory, but it doesn’t work perfectly because hash functions can have collisions. Collisions are when two different keys hash to the same index.
2 1 1 To avoid collisions, we use techniques such as chaining or open addressing.

instruction: For example, say hash(key) = 123456. If you initially have 10 nodes, that key starts out on node 6 (because 123456 mod 10 = 6). When you grow to 11 nodes, the key needs to move to node 3 (123456 mod 11 = 3), and when you grow to 12 nodes, it needs to move to node 0 (123456 mod 12 = 0).

The key with hash value 123456 needs to move to node 7 (123456 mod 11 = 7) when we grow from 10 to 11 nodes.

instruction: However, each partition also has management overhead, so it’s counterproductive to choose too high a number. Choosing the right number of partitions is difficult if the total size of the dataset is highly variable (for example, if it starts small but may grow much larger over time).
output:
One rule of thumb that has been proposed is to choose the number of partitions proportional to the square root of the total number of records. This works well if the dataset size is more or less static.

If the dataset size is highly variable, a better approach might be to use dynamic partitioning. With dynamic partitioning, the database management system automatically adds or removes partitions as the dataset size changes. This can help minimize the management overhead and keep the number of partitions optimal.

instruction: Rebalancing Partitions | 211 Dynamic partitioning For databases that use key range partitioning (see “Partitioning by Key Range” on page 202), a fixed number of partitions with fixed boundaries would be very incon‐ venient: if you got the boundaries wrong, you could end up with all of the data in one partition and all of the other partitions empty.
output:
Dynamic partitioning is a solution to this problem. With dynamic partitioning, the number and size of partitions can be changed as needed. Partitions can be split and merged automatically. The database system manages the partitioning for you.

For example, if a partition becomes too large, it can be split into two smaller partitions. If two partitions are nearly empty, they can be merged to save space. The database system decides when to split or merge partitions based on rules you define.

instruction: Operations: Automatic or Manual Rebalancing There is one important question with regard to rebalancing that we have glossed over: does the rebalancing happen automatically or manually? There is a gradient between fully automatic rebalancing (the system decides automat‐ ically when to move partitions from one node to another, without any administrator interaction) and fully manual (the assignment of partitions to nodes is explicitly con‐ figured by an administrator, and only changes when the administrator explicitly reconfigures it).
output:
The default is usually set to automatic rebalancing, where the system makes decisions regarding when to rebalance on its own, but the administrator can override this choice and set the rebalancing to manual. The choice between automatic and manual rebalancing depends on various factors, including the size and complexity of the cluster, the desired level of control that the administrator has, and the availability of resources for manual intervention.

For large clusters, automatic rebalancing is usually the preferred choice. It allows for more efficient use of resources, since the system can make decisions about where to move partitions based on their current usage and availability. However, for smaller clusters, manual rebalancing may be preferred in order to ensure that the administrator has more control over the placement of partitions.

In summary, the choice between automatic and manual rebalancing is a trade-off between efficiency and control, and the best choice depends on the specific requirements of the cluster and the preferences of the administrator.

instruction: For example, Couchbase, Riak, and Voldemort generate a suggested partition assignment automatically, but require an administrator to commit it before it takes effect. Fully automated rebalancing can be convenient, because there is less operational work to do for normal maintenance.
output:
However, a fully automated partitioning strategy can sometimes lead to less efficient use of resources, especially when data is skewed. For instance, if all the data in a Couchbase cluster is stored in a single partition, then that partition will have to handle all the read and write requests, and the other partitions will be idle. This can result in performance degradation for the partition with all the data, and underutilization of the other partitions.

A more efficient partitioning strategy is to distribute data across multiple partitions evenly, so that each partition handles a roughly equal number of read and write requests. This strategy is known as horizontal partitioning. Horizontal partitioning can lead to more efficient use of resources, because each partition can focus on a subset of the data, and other partitions can focus on different subsets.

If all data in a Couchbase cluster is stored in a single partition, what can be the result in terms of performance and resource utilization?

Why is it important to distribute data across multiple partitions in database systems

instruction: Rebalancing Partitions | 213 Such automation can be dangerous in combination with automatic failure detection. For example, say one node is overloaded and is temporarily slow to respond to requests.
output:
An automated rebalancing process might move some of the node's partitions to other nodes to free up resources and improve overall system performance. However, if the node comes back online before the partitions have been moved, the system is left with partitions on two nodes, which can lead to data inconsistencies and other problems.

To prevent this issue, systems can be designed to implement a coordinated, manual rebalancing process. For example, a human operator can monitor the system's performance, initiate a rebalancing process, and ensure that no partitions are moved until all nodes are ready.

instruction: Request Routing We have now partitioned our dataset across multiple nodes running on multiple machines. But there remains an open question: when a client wants to make a request, how does it know which node to connect to?
output:
To answer this question, we employ a distributed request routing solution. Each client sends its request to a request router. The request router uses a consistent hashing algorithm to determine which node is responsible for handling the request. The result is a simple, scalable, and efficient solution that allows our system to handle a large number of concurrent requests.

instruction: As partitions are rebalanced, the assignment of partitions to nodes changes. Somebody needs to stay on top of those changes in order to answer the question: if I want to read or write the key “foo”, which IP address and port number do I need to connect to?
output:
The DNS-based service, named "Hadoop-NAMENode-Service", can help answer this question.
It responds to queries about the IP addresses and port numbers for keys in the Hadoop cluster.

In a typical cluster setup, there's a single Hadoop NameNode managing the metadata for all Hadoop DataNodes.
The DNS service is configured to point to the IP address of the Hadoop NameNode, so when we query it for the key "foo", it will return the IP address and port number of the DataNode where the key "foo" is currently assigned.

instruction: This is an instance of a more general problem called service discovery, which isn’t limited to just databases. Any piece of software that is accessible over a network has this problem, especially if it is aiming for high availability (running in a redundant configuration on multiple machines).
output:
The solution to the service discovery problem is to use a registry that maintains a list of all available services and their locations. This registry can be implemented in a number of ways, including using a centralized server, a distributed hash table, or even a peer-to-peer network.

The most common way to implement a service registry today is as a centralized server. This is because the server model is easy to understand, easy to deploy, and easy to manage. The downside is that it has a single point of failure. If the server goes down, no one can find the services, even if they are still running.

One approach to mitigating the risk of a single point of failure is to use a high-availability (HA) configuration, which typically involves running multiple copies of the registry on different machines. If one server goes down, the others continue to function. This configuration is typically implemented using load balancing, which distributes requests evenly across the servers.

Question 1:
How is the service discovery problem typically solved?
Answer: The service discovery problem is typically solved by using a registry that maintains a list of all available services and their locations.

instruction: In all cases, the key problem is: how does the component making the routing decision (which may be one of the nodes, or the routing tier, or the client) learn about changes in the assignment of partitions to nodes?
output:
A solution to this problem is to have the component that makes the routing decision (the routing tier) periodically poll the metadata server that manages the partitions, to determine whether any partitions have been reassigned.

The metadata server, which is typically implemented as a distributed hash table, will maintain a mapping from partitions to nodes. When a partition is reassigned, the metadata server will update its mapping accordingly. The routing tier will poll the metadata server to learn about the new assignments.

This approach has the advantage of being simple and efficient. It does not require the routing tier to keep track of the assignment of partitions to nodes, and it does not require the nodes to report their partitions to the routing tier. Instead, the routing tier simply queries the metadata server to learn about the current assignments.

instruction: 214 | Chapter 6: Partitioning Figure 6-7. Three different ways of routing a request to the right node. This is a challenging problem, because it is important that all participants agree— otherwise requests would be sent to the wrong nodes and not handled correctly.
output:
The first way is to use a consistent hashing function, as shown in Figure 6-8. The hash function takes a key as input and returns a fixed number of hash values. Each node is then responsible for handling requests whose hash value falls within its range. The advantages of this method are that it is simple and evenly distributes requests across nodes. The disadvantage is that if a node goes down or is added, all keys have to be rehashed.

The second way is to use a randomized hash function. In this method, the hash function takes a key as input and returns a single hash value. Each node is then responsible for handling requests whose hash value falls within a range defined by the number of nodes and the hash value. The advantages of this method are that it is easy to add or remove nodes, and it distributes requests fairly evenly. The disadvantage is that if multiple keys hash to the same value, requests for those keys will be sent to the same node, which can cause overloading.

instruction: Summary In this chapter we explored different ways of partitioning a large dataset into smaller subsets. Partitioning is necessary when you have so much data that storing and pro‐ cessing it on a single machine is no longer feasible.
output:
We talked about horizontal partitioning, where the dataset is split across multiple machines based on a particular column, and vertical partitioning, where the dataset is split based on different levels of detail.

Horizontal partitioning is typically used when we have a lot of data across many dimensions and want to distribute the data evenly across multiple machines. Vertical partitioning is used when we have data with multiple levels of detail for a single dimension and want to store each level on separate machines.

For example, suppose we have a large dataset of customer information, and we want to distribute the data to store it in multiple machines. Horizontal partitioning would split the dataset into smaller chunks based on a particular column, such as customer_id. Vertical partitioning would split the dataset into smaller chunks based on different levels of detail, such as having one machine for customer names and addresses, another for orders, and another for payment information.

In summary, partitioning is a necessary technique for handling large datasets by distributing the data across multiple machines, and horizontal and vertical partitioning are two common methods for doing so.

instruction: 216 | Chapter 6: Partitioning The goal of partitioning is to spread the data and query load evenly across multiple machines, avoiding hot spots (nodes with disproportionately high load). This requires choosing a partitioning scheme that is appropriate to your data, and reba‐ lancing the partitions when nodes are added to or removed from the cluster.
output:
There are three common partitioning schemes: range, hash, and list. Range partitioning involves dividing the table into partitions based on a range of values, such as date ranges. Hash partitioning uses a hash function to distribute the rows based on the values of the specified partition key. List partitioning involves creating partitions based on a list of values, such as a list of cities.

Each scheme has its advantages and disadvantages. Range partitioning is useful for data that naturally falls into time series or date-based categories, but it can lead to uneven distribution of data if the ranges are not carefully chosen. Hash partitioning is good for data that does not have a natural ordering or is not accessed based on a range, but it can result in hot spots if the hash function does not distribute the data evenly. List partitioning is useful for data that is accessed based on a specific list of values, such as cities, but it can become inefficient if the list is very long.

When deciding on a partitioning scheme, it's important to consider the characteristics of your data and the queries that will be run against it. For example, if your data is primarily accessed based on date ranges, range partitioning may

instruction: However, operations that need to write Summary | 217 to several partitions can be difficult to reason about: for example, what happens if the write to one partition succeeds, but another fails?
output:
In response to this challenge, MapReduce introduces the concept of atomicity, which ensures that operations that need to write to multiple partitions are treated as a single, indivisible unit.

MapReduce achieves atomicity by using a technique called two-phase commit. When a client requests an atomic update, each map and reduce task involved in the update sends a prepare commit message to all other tasks. If all tasks vote to commit, the update is made; if not, the update is aborted, and the client is notified.

This atomicity guarantee allows MapReduce to provide a reliable way for applications to perform complex, multi-partition updates.

instruction: [33] Shivnath Babu and Herodotos Herodotou: “Massively Parallel Databases and MapReduce Systems,” Foundations and Trends in Databases, volume 5, number 1, pages 1–104, November 2013. doi:10.1561/1900000036 Summary | 219
output:
This paper introduces the concept of MapReduce systems and how they differ from
traditional parallel databases. It describes how MapReduce systems perform data
processing tasks using a parallel and distributed approach.

MapReduce systems use a programming model based on a map operation and a reduce operation. The map operation processes a set of input data and produces a set of intermediate key-value pairs. The reduce operation takes those intermediate key-value pairs and combines them into a smaller set of output key-value pairs.

This approach allows MapReduce systems to process large amounts of data in parallel and distribute the workload across multiple machines. Additionally, it provides fault tolerance by replicating the map and reduce tasks across multiple machines, ensuring that the data processing continues even if some machines fail.

instruction: CHAPTER 7 Transactions Some authors have claimed that general two-phase commit is too expensive to support, because of the performance or availability problems that it brings. We believe it is better to have application programmers deal with performance problems due to overuse of transac‐ tions as bottlenecks arise, rather than always coding around the lack of transactions.
output:
In the context of a distributed database, a two-phase commit protocol helps ensure that a database transaction is completed at all participating databases, or none of them. Two-phase commit consists of a prepare phase and a commit phase. The prepare phase checks whether all participating databases agree to the proposed transaction. If all agree, the commit phase is initiated, which finalizes the transaction. If any database disagrees, the transaction is rolled back.

In a nutshell, a two-phase commit protocol is used to ensure that transactions are completed consistently across multiple databases in a distributed system. This helps prevent data inconsistencies and improves data integrity.

instruction: • Several clients may write to the database at the same time, overwriting each other’s changes. • A client may read data that doesn’t make sense because it has only partially been updated.
output:
• To ensure that database transactions are processed correctly, databases use a technique called "concurrency control" that prevents conflicts between transactions.
• Databases can handle concurrency control in two main ways: through optimistic locking or through pessimistic locking.
• Optimistic locking assumes that there won’t be any conflicts, allowing multiple transactions to proceed without locking resources.
• If a conflict arises while using optimistic locking, the database checks the versions of the conflicting transactions and decides which one should be allowed to proceed.
• Pessimistic locking, on the other hand, locks a resource before a transaction begins, ensuring that no other transaction can modify it until the first transaction is complete.
• Both methods have their advantages and disadvantages. Optimistic locking can allow for faster database access, while pessimistic locking ensures stronger consistency.

instruction: • Race conditions between clients can cause surprising bugs. In order to be reliable, a system has to deal with these faults and ensure that they don’t cause catastrophic failure of the entire system.
output:
• When a client sends a request, it’s sent to a queue. When a server is free, it takes the next request from the queue and processes it.
• If two clients try to submit a request at the exact same time, one will win the race and have their request processed before the other. This is known as a race condition.
• One way to deal with race conditions is to use a locking mechanism. The server can check if it already has a lock on a resource before processing a request.
• If a client tries to acquire a lock on a resource that is already locked, it will be blocked and have to wait its turn. This ensures that only one client can change the resource at a time.

Another approach is to use checkpoints. This means regularly saving the state of a system, so that if it crashes, it can be restarted from the last known good state. For example, a database might use checkpoints to ensure that data is not lost if the power goes out.

instruction: With transactions, error handling becomes much simpler for an application, because it doesn’t need to worry about partial failure—i.e., the case where some operations succeed and some fail (for whatever reason).
output:
When a transaction is used, the entire set of operations is treated as an atomic unit, and either the entire transaction succeeds or it fails. If a transaction fails, the entire set of operations is rolled back, and the system is left in the same state as it was before the transaction. This allows an application to recover gracefully from a failed transaction, rather than having to deal with the complexity of partial failure.

For example, if a banking application were to transfer funds between two accounts using a transaction, the transfer of funds would be considered as a single unit. If the transfer of funds from one account succeeded but the transfer to the second account failed, the entire transaction would be rolled back, and both accounts would be left unchanged.

instruction: If you have spent years working with transactions, they may seem obvious, but we shouldn’t take them for granted. Transactions are not a law of nature; they were cre‐ ated with a purpose, namely to simplify the programming model for applications accessing a database.
output:
The first database management systems, those developed in the 1960s and 1970s, didn’t support transactions. Instead, they relied on batch processing, which meant that a program had to write multiple records at once. This was risky, as a power failure or other interruption could easily result in lost or corrupted data.

Transactions came into being when computer scientists figured out a way to ensure the atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability of database updates. Atomicity means that a transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit of work. Consistency means that a database is always left in a valid state, and isolation means that concurrent transactions don’t interfere with each other. Durability ensures that once a transaction is committed, it can’t be rolled back.

instruction: By using transactions, the application is free to ignore certain potential error scenarios and concurrency issues, because the database takes care of them instead (we call these safety guarantees). instruction: Some safety properties can be achieved without transactions. How do you figure out whether you need transactions? In order to answer that ques‐ tion, we first need to understand exactly what safety guarantees transactions can pro‐ vide, and what costs are associated with them.
output:
Transactions offer the following guarantees: serializability, durability, and atomicity. Serializability ensures that the effects of concurrent transactions appear to be the same as those of a single sequential execution. Durability ensures that once a transaction has been committed, it cannot be rolled back, even if power is lost. Atomicity ensures that a transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit.

In terms of cost, transactions impose overheads on the system, such as increased locking, logging, and checkpointing. However, this cost is often worthwhile because transactions provide the essential guarantees that enable complex business applications to operate.

instruction: Although transactions seem straightforward at first glance, there are actually many subtle but important details that come into play. In this chapter, we will examine many examples of things that can go wrong, and explore the algorithms that databases use to guard against those issues.
output:
For instance, consider the problem of transaction ordering. Suppose two customers, Alice and Bob, both try to transfer money to the same account at the same time. Who gets to go first? It's important to make sure that these transactions are processed in a consistent order, because the outcome of the first transaction might depend on the outcome of the second one.

For example, if Alice transfers $100 to an account and then Bob transfers $50 to the same account, we would want the net change to the account to be $150, not $150, then $100. This can be solved by using a method called two-phase locking, which ensures that one transaction is completely finished before the next one starts.

Another example is the problem of dealing with failed transactions. If a transaction cannot be completed for some reason, it must be rolled back to its original state. This is necessary to maintain the integrity of the database.

For example, suppose Alice transfers $100 to a checking account, but the account doesn't have enough money. In this case, the transaction must be rolled back, and the $100 that was debited from Alice'

instruction: This chapter applies to both single-node and distributed databases; in Chapter 8 we will focus the discussion on the particular challenges that arise only in distributed systems. The Slippery Concept of a Transaction Almost all relational databases today, and some nonrelational databases, support transactions.
output:
A transaction is a logical unit of work. It is the smallest unit of work in a database that can be reliably committed or rolled back.

The transaction concept is slippery because of its multiple interpretations. A transaction may include a single SQL statement, or a series of SQL statements.

When a transaction consists of a single statement, it is called a one-phase transaction, also known as a unit of work. When a transaction includes multiple statements, it is called a two-phase transaction.

The two-phase transaction involves a prepare phase and a commit phase. During the prepare phase, the database locks the data needed for the transaction and checks for conflicts. During the commit phase, the database writes the changes to the transaction log and the data.

The transaction concept is used to ensure the consistency of the data in a database. When a transaction is committed, the database ensures that the data is left in a consistent state.

instruction: In order to understand those trade-offs, let’s go into the details of the guarantees that transactions can provide—both in normal operation and in various extreme (but realistic) circumstances. The Meaning of ACID The safety guarantees provided by transactions are often described by the wellknown acronym ACID, which stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Dura‐ bility.
output:
Atomicity ensures that a transaction is treated as a single, indivisible unit of work. Consistency ensures that a transaction leaves the database in a valid state. Isolation ensures that concurrent transactions do not interfere with one another. Durability ensures that the effects of a completed transaction are permanent.

To illustrate, consider the example of a bank transfer from account A to account B, which is implemented as two transactions: T1, which debits account A, and T2, which credits account B. In normal operation, both transactions are executed atomically: they are either both committed or both rolled back. If an error occurs, the entire transfer is rolled back, leaving both accounts unchanged.

However, in case of failures, the situation becomes more complicated. Suppose that T1 is committed but T2 is rolled back. This leaves account A debited and account B unchanged, which violates the basic principles of accounting. To prevent this, the database system uses techniques such as two-phase commit and write-ahead logging.

instruction: For example, as we shall see, there is a lot of ambiguity around the meaning of isolation [8]. The high-level idea is sound, but the devil is in the details.

Some people believe that isolation means being completely cut off from the outside world,
with no contact whatsoever. Others believe that it can simply mean being alone,
or having little contact with others. Still others argue that it can mean being
separated from others in some specific way, such as being in solitary confinement.

The term is used in many fields, including biology, physics, psychology, and social sciences.
It is important to note that its meaning can vary depending on the context.

In the context of this book, when we speak of isolation, we mean being separate from others in some way,
but not necessarily in a negative sense. We may be referring to the isolation of a researcher in a lab,
or the isolation of a monk in a monastery. The key idea is that the person is separated from the larger society,

instruction: Without atomicity, if an error occurs partway through making multiple changes, it’s difficult to know which changes have taken effect and which haven’t. The application could try again, but that risks making the same change twice, leading to duplicate or incorrect data.
output:
One solution to this problem is to use atomic transactions. An atomic transaction is a single unit of work that is guaranteed to complete entirely or not at all. If any step within the transaction fails, the entire transaction is rolled back, leaving the database in its original state. This ensures data consistency and avoids the risk of duplicate or incorrect data.

Another solution, used in some databases, is to employ optimistic concurrency control. With optimistic concurrency control, the database allows multiple transactions to modify the same data at the same time, but checks for conflicts before committing the changes. If a conflict arises, the transaction that lost is rolled back, and the winning transaction is allowed to commit. This approach can improve performance since it does not require the locking and waiting associated with atomic transactions. However, it does add the complexity of checking for conflicts and handling rolled-back transactions.

instruction: 224 | Chapter 7: Transactions The idea of ACID consistency is that you have certain statements about your data (invariants) that must always be true—for example, in an accounting system, credits and debits across all accounts must always be balanced.
output:
ACID consistency is achieved through a set of database properties: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability.

Atomicity: All changes to a database are treated as a single unit of work. If any part of the transaction fails, the entire transaction is rolled back to its previous state.

Consistency: The database must maintain a consistent state. Before a transaction is accepted, the database checks that no constraints are violated, and that all data is valid.

Isolation: Transactions are isolated from one another. They cannot interfere with one another.

Durability: Once a transaction is committed, it is guaranteed to remain in the database. The database writes a log entry for each transaction, and the log is written to disk.
