In the example just given, uniqueness and monotonic sequence are safety properties, but availability is a liveness property. What distinguishes the two kinds of properties? A giveaway is that liveness properties often include the word “eventually” in their definition. (And yes, you guessed it— eventual consistency is a liveness property [89].) Safety is often informally defined as nothing bad happens, and liveness as something good eventually happens. However, it’s best to not read too much into those informal definitions, because the meaning of good and bad is subjective. The actual definitions of safety and liveness are precise and mathematical [90]: 308 | Chapter 8: The Trouble with Distributed Systems • If a safety property is violated, we can point at a particular point in time at which it was broken (for example, if the uniqueness property was violated, we can iden‐ tify the particular operation in which a duplicate fencing token was returned). After a safety property has been violated, the violation cannot be undone—the damage is already done. • A liveness property works the other way round: it may not hold at some point in time (for example, a node may have sent a request but not yet received a response), but there is always hope that it may be satisfied in the future (namely by receiving a response). An advantage of distinguishing between safety and liveness properties is that it helps us deal with difficult system models. For distributed algorithms, it is common to require that safety properties always hold, in all possible situations of a system model [88]. That is, even if all nodes crash, or the entire network fails, the algorithm must nevertheless ensure that it does not return a wrong result (i.e., that the safety proper‐ ties remain satisfied). However, with liveness properties we are allowed to make caveats: for example, we could say that a request needs to receive a response only if a majority of nodes have not crashed, and only if the network eventually recovers from an outage. The defini‐ tion of the partially synchronous model requires that eventually the system returns to a synchronous state—that is, any period of network interruption lasts only for a finite duration and is then repaired. Mapping system models to the real world Safety and liveness properties and system models are very useful for reasoning about the correctness of a distributed algorithm. However, when implementing an algo‐ rithm in practice, the messy facts of reality come back to bite you again, and it becomes clear that the system model is a simplified abstraction of reality. For example, algorithms in the crash-recovery model generally assume that data in stable storage survives crashes. However, what happens if the data on disk is corrup‐ ted, or the data is wiped out due to hardware error or misconfiguration [91]? What happens if a server has a firmware bug and fails to recognize its hard drives on reboot, even though the drives are correctly attached to the server [92]? Quorum algorithms (see “Quorums for reading and writing” on page 179) rely on a node remembering the data that it claims to have stored. If a node may suffer from amnesia and forget previously stored data, that breaks the quorum condition, and thus breaks the correctness of the algorithm. Perhaps a new system model is needed, in which we assume that stable storage mostly survives crashes, but may sometimes be lost. But that model then becomes harder to reason about. Knowledge, Truth, and Lies | 309 The theoretical description of an algorithm can declare that certain things are simply assumed not to happen—and in non-Byzantine systems, we do have to make some assumptions about faults that can and cannot happen. However, a real implementa‐ tion may still have to include code to handle the case where something happens that was assumed to be impossible, even if that handling boils down to printf("Sucks to be you") and exit(666)—i.e., letting a human operator clean up the mess [93]. (This is arguably the difference between computer science and software engineering.) That is not to say that theoretical, abstract system models are worthless—quite the opposite. They are incredibly helpful for distilling down the complexity of real sys‐ tems to a manageable set of faults that we can reason about, so that we can under‐ stand the problem and try to solve it systematically. We can prove algorithms correct by showing that their properties always hold in some system model. Proving an algorithm correct does not mean its implementation on a real system will necessarily always behave correctly. But it’s a very good first step, because the theo‐ retical analysis can uncover problems in an algorithm that might remain hidden for a long time in a real system, and that only come to bite you when your assumptions (e.g., about timing) are defeated due to unusual circumstances. Theoretical analysis and empirical testing are equally important. Summary In this chapter we have discussed a wide range of problems that can occur in dis‐ tributed systems, including: • Whenever you try to send a packet over the network, it may be lost or arbitrarily delayed. Likewise, the reply may be lost or delayed, so if you don’t get a reply, you have no idea whether the message got through. • A node’s clock may be significantly out of sync with other nodes (despite your best efforts to set up NTP), it may suddenly jump forward or back in time, and relying on it is dangerous because you most likely don’t have a good measure of your clock’s error interval. • A process may pause for a substantial amount of time at any point in its execu‐ tion (perhaps due to a stop-the-world garbage collector), be declared dead by other nodes, and then come back to life again without realizing that it was paused. The fact that such partial failures can occur is the defining characteristic of dis‐ tributed systems. Whenever software tries to do anything involving other nodes, there is the possibility that it may occasionally fail, or randomly go slow, or not respond at all (and eventually time out). In distributed systems, we try to build toler‐ 310 | Chapter 8: The Trouble with Distributed Systems ance of partial failures into software, so that the system as a whole may continue functioning even when some of its constituent parts are broken. To tolerate faults, the first step is to detect them, but even that is hard. Most systems don’t have an accurate mechanism of detecting whether a node has failed, so most distributed algorithms rely on timeouts to determine whether a remote node is still available. However, timeouts can’t distinguish between network and node failures, and variable network delay sometimes causes a node to be falsely suspected of crash‐ ing. Moreover, sometimes a node can be in a degraded state: for example, a Gigabit network interface could suddenly drop to 1 Kb/s throughput due to a driver bug [94]. Such a node that is “limping” but not dead can be even more difficult to deal with than a cleanly failed node. Once a fault is detected, making a system tolerate it is not easy either: there is no global variable, no shared memory, no common knowledge or any other kind of shared state between the machines. Nodes can’t even agree on what time it is, let alone on anything more profound. The only way information can flow from one node to another is by sending it over the unreliable network. Major decisions cannot be safely made by a single node, so we require protocols that enlist help from other nodes and try to get a quorum to agree. If you’re used to writing software in the idealized mathematical perfection of a single computer, where the same operation always deterministically returns the same result, then moving to the messy physical reality of distributed systems can be a bit of a shock. Conversely, distributed systems engineers will often regard a problem as triv‐ ial if it can be solved on a single computer [5], and indeed a single computer can do a lot nowadays [95]. If you can avoid opening Pandora’s box and simply keep things on a single machine, it is generally worth doing so. However, as discussed in the introduction to Part II, scalability is not the only reason for wanting to use a distributed system. Fault tolerance and low latency (by placing data geographically close to users) are equally important goals, and those things can‐ not be achieved with a single node. In this chapter we also went on some tangents to explore whether the unreliability of networks, clocks, and processes is an inevitable law of nature. We saw that it isn’t: it is possible to give hard real-time response guarantees and bounded delays in net‐ works, but doing so is very expensive and results in lower utilization of hardware resources. Most non-safety-critical systems choose cheap and unreliable over expen‐ sive and reliable. We also touched on supercomputers, which assume reliable components and thus have to be stopped and restarted entirely when a component does fail. By contrast, distributed systems can run forever without being interrupted at the service level, because all faults and maintenance can be handled at the node level—at least in Summary | 311 theory. (In practice, if a bad configuration change is rolled out to all nodes, that will still bring a distributed system to its knees.) This chapter has been all about problems, and has given us a bleak outlook. In the next chapter we will move on to solutions, and discuss some algorithms that have been designed to cope with all the problems in distributed systems. CHAPTER 9 Consistency and Consensus Is it better to be alive and wrong or right and dead? —Jay Kreps, A Few Notes on Kafka and Jepsen (2013) Lots of things can go wrong in distributed systems, as discussed in Chapter 8.
output:
In this chapter, we focus on consistency models, which are crucial for dealing with failures.
Consistency models guarantee that the state of a distributed system remains consistent, even when network partitions or failures occur.
There are three main consistency models:
- Strong consistency: Every read operation returns the most recent write, even if it came from a different node.
- Eventual consistency: Every read operation eventually returns the most recent write, but it may take some time.
- Weak consistency: There's no guarantee that read operations return the most recent write.

Questions:
1. What are the three main consistency models for dealing with failures in distributed systems?
2. Why does Jay Kreps focus on consistency models in Chapter 9 of his book?
3. How does the strong consistency model ensure that every read operation returns the most recent write?

instruction: The simplest way of handling such faults is to simply let the entire service fail, and show the user an error message. If that solution is unacceptable, we need to find ways of tolerating faults—that is, of keeping the service functioning correctly, even if some internal component is faulty.
output:
One popular method for dealing with faults is to employ redundancy—that is, to have multiple copies of a component in place, and to use a voting algorithm or other means to determine which copy to use. Another approach is to use a load balancer to distribute requests to multiple instances of a service. A third approach is to use a circuit breaker pattern, which monitors the state of a service, and if it detects that the service is failing, stops sending requests to it and instead returns a fallback value to the user.

Passage:
In this chapter, we'll discuss how to handle faults in distributed systems. We'll cover three approaches: using redundancy, using a load balancer, and using a circuit breaker pattern.

Question 1:
What are three methods for dealing with faults in distributed systems mentioned in the chapter?

Question 2:
Why is employing redundancy an effective way to handle faults in distributed systems?

Question 3:

instruction: In this chapter, we will talk about some examples of algorithms and protocols for building fault-tolerant distributed systems. We will assume that all the problems from Chapter 8 can occur: packets can be lost, reordered, duplicated, or arbitrarily delayed in the network; clocks are approximate at best; and nodes can pause (e.g., due to garbage collection) or crash at any time.
output:
One of the simplest protocols for handling lost packets is the Stop-and-Wait protocol. In this protocol, the sender waits for an acknowledgment (ACK) from the receiver before sending the next packet. This ensures that the sender does not send a packet that has already been lost or received out of order.

Another protocol is the Go-Back-N protocol, which builds on the Stop-and-Wait protocol. In Go-Back-N, the sender sends N packets and then waits for an ACK for the first packet. Once the ACK is received, the sender can send the next N packets. This reduces the overhead of waiting for an ACK for each packet to N/2 on average. However, if a packet is lost, the sender has to resend all the packets after the lost packet.

A third protocol is the Selective Repeat protocol, which also builds on the Stop-and-Wait protocol. In Selective Repeat, the receiver sends an ACK for each packet it receives correctly. The sender then resends only the packets that were not acknowledged, reducing the amount of data that needs to be resent in case of packet loss.

instruction: The best way of building fault-tolerant systems is to find some general-purpose abstractions with useful guarantees, implement them once, and then let applications rely on those guarantees. This is the same approach as we used with transactions in Chapter 7: by using a transaction, the application can pretend that there are no crashes (atomicity), that nobody else is concurrently accessing the database (isola‐ tion), and that storage devices are perfectly reliable (durability).

instruction: As discussed in “Handling Node Outages” on page 156, it’s important that there is only one leader, and that all nodes agree who the leader is. If two nodes both believe that they are the leader, that situation is called split brain, and it often leads to data loss.
output:
To prevent split brain, nodes elect a new leader if they detect that two leaders currently exist. When a node determines that it is no longer the leader, it sends a message to all other nodes announcing its departure. The remaining nodes then elect a new leader.

This process of electing a new leader can take some time, particularly when there are many nodes involved. However, it is necessary for maintaining the integrity of the system.

instruction: But first we first need to explore the range of guarantees and abstractions that can be provided in a distributed system. We need to understand the scope of what can and cannot be done: in some situa‐ tions, it’s possible for the system to tolerate faults and continue working; in other sit‐ uations, that is not possible.
output:
We can classify the guarantees provided by a distributed system into three main categories:

1. Strong consistency: every read operation returns the most recent write or an error.
2. Eventual consistency: every read operation returns some value, but not necessarily the most recent write.
3. Weak consistency: no guarantees are made about the order or consistency of read/write operations.

instruction: The limits of what is and isn’t possible have been explored in depth, both in theoretical proofs and in practical implementations. We will get an overview of those fundamental limits in this chapter.
output:
One of the most famous and important results in computer science is the Church-Turing Thesis, which essentially says that all computation can be performed by a Turing machine. This machine is a theoretical model that can simulate any computation that can be performed by a modern computer. The Church-Turing Thesis is a statement about the power and capabilities of computation. It does not dictate the physical limits of computers, only the theoretical ones.

Another fundamental concept in computer science is the halting problem. This problem asks whether, given any program and its input, it is possible to determine whether the program will eventually halt or continue to run indefinitely. This question was proven to be undecidable by Alan Turing in 1936. The halting problem is a key concept in understanding the limits of computation. It shows that there are certain questions that cannot be answered definitively by any computer program.

instruction: Researchers in the field of distributed systems have been studying these topics for decades, so there is a lot of material—we’ll only be able to scratch the surface. In this book we don’t have space to go into details of the formal models and proofs, so we will stick with informal intuitions.
output:
Distributed systems are those in which components, or nodes, located on networked computers communicate and coordinate with each other by passing messages. These messages are sent along communication channels, which may be wired or wireless, and may be unreliable.

The primary challenge in distributed systems is dealing with this unreliability. There are many ways that the communication channels can fail: they can become congested or saturated, they can lose packets of data, or they can even go down completely.

Nodes in distributed systems are also prone to failing, and when they do, the system must continue to function. To this end, many distributed systems employ redundancy, meaning that there are multiple copies of each component or node in the system.

How do distributed systems deal with the challenge of unreliable communication channels?
What causes nodes in distributed systems to fail and how is the system designed to continue functioning?

instruction: The literature references offer plenty of additional depth if you’re interested. Consistency Guarantees In “Problems with Replication Lag” on page 161 we looked at some timing issues that occur in a replicated database.
output:
The author explains that in a replicated database, updates are sent from one node to another, and if those updates conflict with updates on the target node, then a merge operation is required to ensure consistency. This process can be complex and time-consuming. The author also mentions that the merge operation is often delayed, leading to a problem called replication lag.

2. How does a merge operation work to ensure consistency in a replicated database?

instruction: If you look at two database nodes at the same moment in time, you’re likely to see different data on the two nodes, because write requests arrive on different nodes at different times.
output:
This is due to the eventual consistency model that the database employs. When a write request comes in, it is not immediately propagated to every node. Instead, it is replicated asynchronously, meaning that it may take some time before the write is visible on all nodes.

However, this delay can be minimized by increasing the number of replicas that the database maintains. The more replicas you have, the faster the propagation of writes.

Most replicated databases provide at least eventual consistency, which means that if you stop writing to the database and wait for some unspecified length of time, then eventually all read requests will return the same value [1].

instruction: 322 | Chapter 9: Consistency and Consensus However, this is a very weak guarantee—it doesn’t say anything about when the repli‐ cas will converge. Until the time of convergence, reads could return anything or nothing [1].

323 | The problem of consensus can be alleviated by using a consensus algorithm, such as Paxos [2] or Raft [3]. A consensus algorithm ensures that all members agree on the same state, even if some nodes fail.

324 | The Paxos algorithm works by electing a proposer node that proposes a value for a given log entry. The proposer sends a request to a majority of nodes, asking them to vote for the value. Once a majority of nodes have voted, the proposer announces the value and becomes the leader.

instruction: Eventual consistency is hard for application developers because it is so different from the behavior of variables in a normal single-threaded program. If you assign a value to a variable and then read it shortly afterward, you don’t expect to read back the old value, or for the read to fail.
output:
But with eventual consistency, you can’t assume that reading back a value shortly after writing it will return the new value. Instead, you must accept that, after a write, there might be a delay before the new value can be read back.

If you write to a variable and read it back a few seconds later and still don’t see the new value, you might panic, thinking there is a problem with the system. But eventual consistency is a fundamental property of distributed systems and cannot be avoided. The only way to deal with it is to accept that there will be a delay and to design your application accordingly.

instruction: A database looks superficially like a variable that you can read and write, but in fact it has much more complicated semantics [3]. When working with a database that provides only weak guarantees, you need to be constantly aware of its limitations and not accidentally assume too much.
output:
For instance, you might think that you can read the value of a record and write it to another record, but a database may not allow concurrent writes to the same record. This means that if you try to write to a record that another process is currently writing to, the outcome is undefined.

Another limitation is that databases do not have an intrinsic concept of time, so you cannot assume that you can read the value of a record and then write it back later with the assumption that nothing has changed. This is known as the "write skew" problem.

In summary, databases are complex data structures with their own set of rules and limitations, and working with them requires a good understanding of their semantics.

instruction: In this chapter we will explore stronger consistency models that data systems may choose to provide. They don’t come for free: systems with stronger guarantees may have worse performance or be less fault-tolerant than systems with weaker guaran‐ tees.
output:
One such model is called "linearizability." It promises that all operations, even concurrent ones, appear to have executed atomically, without any interleaving. This means that any two conflicting operations, like a debit and a credit on the same account, will always be resolved in the same order across all copies of the data, even if the operations were sent to the system at different times.

Another stronger consistency model is "sequential consistency." It requires that all operations, again even concurrent ones, appear to be executed in some total order. However, unlike linearizability, it does not guarantee that conflicting operations will be resolved in any particular order.

These models provide stronger guarantees for data systems but also have trade-offs in performance and fault tolerance.

instruction: Consistency Guarantees | 323 Linearizability In an eventually consistent database, if you ask two different replicas the same ques‐ tion at the same time, you may get two different answers.
output:
Linearizability, on the other hand, ensures that no two conflicting operations occur at the same time.

For example, suppose two transactions t1 and t2 both try to update a shared variable x at the same time, and t1 reads the value of x before t2 starts. In an eventually consistent database, t1 might see the old value of x, while t2 might see the updated value, leading to inconsistent results. However, in a linearizable database, t1 will always see the updated value, and t2 will be forced to wait until t1 has completed.

In summary, linearizability guarantees that conflicting updates cannot overlap, while eventually consistency allows them to. The choice between the two depends on your specific use case and the tradeoffs you are willing to make.

instruction: That’s confusing. Wouldn’t it be a lot simpler if the database could give the illusion that there is only one replica (i.e., only one copy of the data)? Then every client would have the same view of the data, and you wouldn’t have to worry about replication lag.
output:
Unfortunately, that’s not possible. The database can’t give the illusion of a single copy when there are actually multiple copies. This is because databases need to be able to write to the data, and writing to multiple copies at the same time is a complex operation that can lead to inconsistencies.

However, there is a solution. Instead of trying to hide the fact that there are multiple copies, databases use a technique called multiversion concurrency control (MVCC) to manage the data so that every client sees a consistent view of the data, even though there are multiple copies.

Here's how it works: Each time a write operation is performed, a new version of the data is created. The database keeps track of all the versions of the data, and each client sees the version of the data that was committed at the time it connected to the database. This means that even if a write operation is performed on one replica, other clients will continue to see the previous version of the data until they reconnect to the database and get the new version.

Question 1:
How does a database manage to provide each client with a consistent view of the data despite having multiple copies?

instruction: This is the idea behind linearizability [6] (also known as atomic consistency [7], strong consistency, immediate consistency, or external consistency [8]). The exact definition of linearizability is quite subtle, and we will explore it in the rest of this section.
output:
A linearizable system is one in which all operations appear to have taken place atomically, even though they may have been executed concurrently. This means that if one operation appears to have completed before another, then it truly did complete before.

Linearizability is often described in terms of the "happened-before" relation, which we define as follows:

* An operation A happens before operation B if and only if every execution that includes A also includes B, and no execution that includes B includes A.

Intuitively, this means that if operation A causes the effect of changing some state, and operation B observes that change, then A happened before B.

For example, suppose we have a shared counter that two processes increment. According to the happened-before relation, any operation that reads the counter after an operation that increments it must have seen the incremented value. In other words, if process A increments the counter, and then process B reads the counter, we say that A happened before B.

This is the fundamental guarantee of linearizability: if one operation appears to have happened before another, then it truly did. This guarantee is important because it allows us to reason about concurrent

instruction: But the basic idea is to make a system appear as if there were only one copy of the data, and all operations on it are atomic. With this guarantee, even though there may be multiple replicas in reality, the application does not need to worry about them.

instruction: In the distributed systems literature, x is called a register—in practice, it could be one key in a key-value store, one row in a relational database, or one document in a document database, for example.

In the case of a distributed database, a single record (or row) is replicated across multiple nodes in the cluster, and each node maintains a copy of the record.

When an update to the record is requested, the client node sends the request to one of the nodes in the cluster, which then applies the update to its copy of the record and sends a message to all other nodes in the cluster to update their copies as well.

This process is known as "replication," and it's a fundamental mechanism for ensuring data consistency in a distributed database.

output:
To ensure that a write is visible to all readers, it must be atomic, meaning that no other operation can start before it completes. This is known as sequential consistency.

Moreover, it's important to note that sequential consistency is not the same as strong consistency. Strong consistency means that once a write is committed, the new value is immediately visible to all readers. However, with sequential consistency, a write can be committed and still not visible to all readers. This is because, while a write must be atomic and completed before any other operation can start, there's no requirement for the read and write to happen in the same order on all nodes.

instruction: Linearizability Versus Serializability Linearizability is easily confused with serializability (see “Serializability” on page 251), as both words seem to mean something like “can be arranged in a sequential order.” However, they are two quite different guarantees, and it is important to distinguish between them: Serializability Serializability is an isolation property of transactions, where every transaction may read and write multiple objects (rows, documents, records)—see “SingleObject and Multi-Object Operations” on page 228.
output:
Serializability ensures that the system appears to execute transactions as if they were executed atomically, without interleaving. This means that the outcome of one transaction cannot affect the outcome of another transaction.
Linearizability Linearizability is a stronger consistency model than serializability. Linearizability guarantees that every operation appears to execute atomically, even if it doesn’t. This means that there is no interleaving of operations.

instruction: Linearizability | 329 Relying on Linearizability In what circumstances is linearizability useful? Viewing the final score of a sporting match is perhaps a frivolous example: a result that is outdated by a few seconds is unlikely to cause any real harm in this situation.
output:
However, when dealing with financial transactions or updates to databases, ensuring that operations are atomic and linearizable is crucial. In the financial sector, transactions need to be processed in a specific order and the outcome of each transaction must not affect the outcome of any other. A financial institution, for instance, cannot afford to process two competing buy orders for the same stock simultaneously, as this could result in double charges and significant financial losses.

Similarly, databases must be updated in a linearizable way to ensure data consistency. For example, consider a banking application that needs to update two records in a database: a customer's account balance and a transaction record. If these updates are not linearizable, the application may end up with incorrect data: either the transaction record will be missing, or the customer's account balance will be incorrect.

In both these cases, the consequences of non-linearizability could be severe, leading to financial losses, incorrect data, or other unwanted outcomes.

instruction: Constraints and uniqueness guarantees Uniqueness constraints are common in databases: for example, a username or email address must uniquely identify one user, and in a file storage service there cannot be two files with the same path and filename.
output:
SQL databases provide the following guarantees:

1. Atomicity: All transactions are guaranteed to be completed in their entirety or not at all.
2. Consistency: The database is guaranteed to be in a consistent state after each transaction.
3. Isolation: Transactions are guaranteed to be executed in isolation from one another.
4. Durability: Once a transaction has been committed, it cannot be rolled back.

These guarantees ensure that uniqueness constraints are maintained, even when multiple transactions are running concurrently.

Example: Two transactions attempt to insert a row with the same username 'alice'. Since both transactions are isolated from one another, each thinks that it alone is inserting a new row and the database does not detect a conflict, allowing both transactions to complete. However, because the database guarantees atomicity, only one of the transactions will actually be added to the database while the other is rolled back, maintaining the uniqueness constraint.

Question 1: What guarantee ensures that databases maintain uniqueness constraints even when multiple transactions are running concurrently?
Answer: The guarantees of atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability ensure that databases maintain uniqueness

instruction: If you want to enforce this constraint as the data is written (such that if two people try to concurrently create a user or a file with the same name, one of them will be returned an error), you need linearizability.
output:
Linearizability is a consistency model that ensures that all concurrent operations appear to have taken place atomically, in some order, even if they did not necessarily execute in that order. It is the strongest consistency model for concurrent data structures.

Linearizable data structures implement compare-and-swap (CAS) operations, which are used to atomically update a value if and only if it matches the expected value.

CAS operations work by attempting to update a memory location with a new value only if the current value is the same as the expected value. If the update succeeds, the new value becomes the current value, and the CAS operation returns true. If the update fails, the CAS operation returns false.

Question 1:

What operation do linearizable data structures use to atomically update a value based on its current value?

instruction: Implementing Linearizable Systems Now that we’ve looked at a few examples in which linearizability is useful, let’s think about how we might implement a system that offers linearizable semantics. Since linearizability essentially means “behave as though there is only a single copy of the data, and all operations on it are atomic,” the simplest answer would be to really only use a single copy of the data.
output:
But that doesn't scale, and it also makes concurrent access impossible. Instead, we implement a linearizable system using a log (or a series of logs, as we’ll see). Each log entry has a timestamp, a sequence number, and some data. When a client makes a request, the server writes a new entry into its log, assigning it a sequence number and a timestamp based on the current system time.

This means that even if multiple clients make requests at the same time, the server will always process them in the order determined by the sequence numbers. If one client’s request is processed after another, then that client’s request was processed later, and all clients will see that reflected in the order of their responses.

So, to implement a linearizable system, we’re essentially just keeping track of the order in which operations are applied to data, and making sure that all clients see those operations in that same order, even if the operations are applied concurrently on the server side.

2. What is the role of the timestamp and sequence number in maintaining linearizability

instruction: 332 | Chapter 9: Consistency and Consensus The most common approach to making a system fault-tolerant is to use replication. Let’s revisit the replication methods from Chapter 5, and compare whether they can be made linearizable: Single-leader replication (potentially linearizable) In a system with single-leader replication (see “Leaders and Followers” on page 152), the leader has the primary copy of the data that is used for writes, and the followers maintain backup copies of the data on other nodes.
output:
The leader election process, which chooses the leader node, can be made consensus-based, ensuring that all nodes agree on who the leader is. When a write request is received at a follower node, it sends an acknowledgement back to the client only after it has confirmed that the write has been replicated to the leader and the leader has acknowledged the write. This process is known as two-phase commit.

333 | The main challenge with single-leader replication is handling failures of the leader itself, because the followers do not have write authority. In a single-leader replication system, if the leader fails, one of the followers must take over as leader, and this can lead to data inconsistency.

Multi-leader replication (not linearizable) In a multi-leader replication system, each node is responsible for handling writes to a portion of the data. The nodes communicate with each other to ensure consistency, but there is no single leader.

Multi-leader replication is not linearizable, because there is no guarantee that writes to the same key will be processed in the same order on all nodes. This means that the system may end up with inconsistent data

instruction: If you make reads from the leader, or from synchronously updated followers, they have the poten‐ tial to be linearizable.iv However, not every single-leader database is actually line‐ arizable, either by design (e.g., because it uses snapshot isolation) or due to concurrency bugs [10].

To ensure linearizability in the presence of network delays, we use a technique called vector clocks. Vector clocks provide a total order on all writes to a given key, even if they are sent to different replicas. This ensures that when a client reads a value, it will read the most recent value that was written.

instruction: The Cost of Linearizability As some replication methods can provide linearizability and others cannot, it is inter‐ esting to explore the pros and cons of linearizability in more depth. We already discussed some use cases for different replication methods in Chapter 5; for example, we saw that multi-leader replication is often a good choice for multidatacenter replication (see “Multi-datacenter operation” on page 168).
output:
However, linearizability comes with a cost. This cost is not just the cost of the synchronization protocol itself. Rather, linearizability can impact the performance and complexity of the application.

For example, to ensure that a write is linearizable, a replica must wait for all other replicas to acknowledge the write before the write can be considered complete. This can lead to additional network latency and increased contention.

In addition, linearizability can require more complex data structures and algorithms, which can impact the memory footprint and CPU usage. For example, a data structure called a register must be used to ensure that a write operation is linearizable, which can be more complex and resource intensive than other data structures.

Lastly, linearizability can impact the consistency model of an application. For example, eventual consistency, which is commonly used in distributed databases, can be a more suitable consistency model for certain applications due to its lower latency and resource requirements.

In a multi-leader setup, writes can be made to any datacenter, and clients can read from any datacenter that has the most up-to-date copy of the database.

However, since datacenters communicate with each other in a distributed manner, a write made to one datacenter might not immediately propagate to other datacenters. The protocols in use ensure that all datacenters will eventually converge to the same state, but the latency of the network and the write propagation time mean that there is a window of opportunity during which the datacenters might have different copies of the database.

This is why a client must always check if the copy of the database that it is reading from is up-to-date before making any changes to the database. The client can do this by querying the metadata associated with the database. If the metadata indicates that the copy of the database is the most up-to-date, then the client can proceed with its write or read operation. If the metadata indicates that the copy is not the most up-to-date, then the client must wait for the most up-to-date copy to become available.

instruction: The CAP theorem This issue is not just a consequence of single-leader and multi-leader replication: any linearizable database has this problem, no matter how it is implemented. The issue also isn’t specific to multi-datacenter deployments, but can occur on any unreliable network, even within one datacenter.
output:
The CAP theorem states that it is impossible for a database system to simultaneously provide all three guarantees: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. The guarantees are defined as follows:
- Consistency: every read receives the most recent write or an error
- Availability: every request receives a response, without guarantee that it contains the most recent version of the information
- Partition tolerance: the system continues to function even when network partitions occur

These guarantees are mutually exclusive: if a database guarantees consistency, it cannot guarantee availability and partition tolerance at the same time. If it guarantees availability, it cannot guarantee consistency and partition tolerance. If it guarantees partition tolerance, it cannot guarantee consistency and availability.

Question 1: Which guarantees does a database system face difficulty in providing simultaneously according to the CAP theorem?

output:
v • If, however, your application can tolerate eventual consistency, then all replicas can continue to process requests while disconnected.

v • In practice, many applications fall in the middle between these two extremes: they require eventual consistency for some operations, but not for others.

instruction: • If your application does not require linearizability, then it can be written in a way that each replica can process requests independently, even if it is disconnected from other replicas (e.g., multi-leader).
output:
• If your application requires strong consistency, then it must be written in a way that ensures all replicas have the same state at all times, which typically requires a consensus protocol (e.g., Raft, Paxos, or ZooKeeper).
• A consensus protocol requires a majority vote, so it can only tolerate up to n/2 failures, assuming n replicas.
• Multi-leader systems, on the other hand, can tolerate up to n/2 failures and do not require a consensus protocol.
• Raft, Paxos, and ZooKeeper are examples of consensus protocols.

instruction: This insight is popularly known as the CAP theorem [29, 30, 31, 32], named by Eric Brewer in 2000, although the trade-off has been known to designers of distributed databases since the 1970s [33, 34, 35, 36].
output:
The CAP theorem states that it is impossible for a distributed database to simultaneously provide all three guarantees: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.

Consistency ensures that every read receives the most recent write or an error. Availability ensures that every request receives a response. Partition tolerance ensures that the database remains available even when network partitions occur.

The theorem is often remembered with the acronym CAAP, where the "P" stands for partition tolerance, but the original theorem only had three guarantees.

Brewer's original formulation of the CAP theorem was that a distributed database could only guarantee two out of three: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.

---

instruction: CAP was originally proposed as a rule of thumb, without precise definitions, with the goal of starting a discussion about trade-offs in databases. instruction: By contrast, a network parti‐ tion is a particular type of network fault, which we normally don’t consider separately from other kinds of faults. However, since it’s the P in CAP, we can’t avoid the confusion in this case.
output:
A network partition is a situation that occurs when a network splits into two or more independent parts, each unaware of the others existence. The simplest example of a network partition is a router that goes down, severing the connection between two network segments.

Another example of a network partition can occur when a link between two switches goes down, resulting in both switches thinking they are the master of the network segment and forwarding frames to each other. This is known as a broadcast storm, and can result in a denial-of-service condition for the entire network segment.

instruction: Linearizability and network delays Although linearizability is a useful guarantee, surprisingly few systems are actually linearizable in practice. For example, even RAM on a modern multi-core CPU is not linearizable [43]: if a thread running on one CPU core writes to a memory address, and a thread on another CPU core reads the same address shortly afterward, it is not guaranteed to read the value written by the first thread (unless a memory barrier or fence [44] is used).
output:
Moreover, even if a distributed system is designed to be linearizable, network delays can make it appear nonlinearizable. For instance, if one process sends a request to another process, and the request takes a long time to propagate across the network, the second process might receive the request after processing another request, resulting in nonlinearizable behavior.

instruction: 338 | Chapter 9: Consistency and Consensus Ordering Guarantees We said previously that a linearizable register behaves as if there is only a single copy of the data, and that every operation appears to take effect atomically at one point in time.
output:
However, there is a subtlety here: while it's true that every operation seems to take effect atomically, it's important to understand that this atomicity is a logical one, not a physical one. In other words, the fact that there appears to be a single copy of the data does not imply that there is actually a single copy in existence. Instead, all copies of the data are kept consistent with one another by a consensus algorithm, which ensures that every copy eventually reflects the most recent state of the data.

For example, consider a distributed database that maintains a counter that is incremented whenever a new record is added. In a linearizable system, the counter is incremented atomically, but this does not imply that there is a single physical copy of the counter in existence. Instead, each server that participates in the database maintains its own copy of the counter, and they all agree on the value of the counter using a consensus algorithm.

instruction: It turns out that there are deep connections between ordering, linearizability, and consensus. Although this notion is a bit more theoretical and abstract than the rest of this book, it is very helpful for clarifying our understanding of what systems can and cannot do.
output:
In the context of distributed databases, ordering refers to a total order on the set of updates to a database. Linearizability, on the other hand, refers to the property that all concurrent operations on a shared resource appear to be executed atomically, as if they were interleaved in some sequential order. Consensus is a protocol that enables a distributed system to agree on a common value.

Consensus is a fundamental building block for many distributed systems. For example, it is used in the Paxos and Raft algorithms for achieving agreement on a common state among distributed nodes. Both Paxos and Raft provide strong guarantees, including safety, liveness, and consistency.

instruction: We will explore this topic in the next few sections. Ordering and Causality There are several reasons why ordering keeps coming up, and one of the reasons is that it helps preserve causality.
output:
Causality is a fundamental concept in logic. It deals with the relationship between cause and effect. When an event causes another event, we say that there is a causal relationship between them.

The ordering of events is important because it helps us understand causality. If we don't know the order of events, it can be difficult to determine which event caused which. For example, if we see a glass shatter on the floor and then see a person stepping on it, it's not immediately clear whether the person stepped on the glass and caused it to shatter, or whether the person stepped on the shattered glass by mistake. By establishing the order of events, we can more easily determine which event caused which.

Another reason why ordering is important is that it helps us make more accurate predictions. If we know the order of events, we can use that information to make predictions about what will happen next. For example, if we know that it always rains after a certain temperature is reached, we can use that information to predict when it will rain based on the temperature.

What is the significance of causality in logic?

Why does establishing the order of events

When a transaction begins, it records its dependencies on earlier transactions, and when it ends, it records the dependencies that were imposed on it. If a transaction writes to a data item that was last written by a concurrent transaction, it must first obtain a lock on that data item. This is called the write skew lock.

The write skew lock is a key part of serializable snapshot isolation. If a transaction requests to go off call and doesn't have a write skew lock, it must wait until the next checkpoint to go off call. The checkpoint is a point in time at which all transactions have been committed or aborted.

instruction: These chains of causally dependent operations define the causal order in the system—i.e., what happened before what. 340 | Chapter 9: Consistency and Consensus If a system obeys the ordering imposed by causality, we say that it is causally consis‐ tent.
output:
For example, consider a system with two processes: P1 and P2. If P1 can send a message to P2 only after P2 has acknowledged receipt of the previous message, then the two processes are causally consistent.

However, if the system is not causally consistent, it can lead to serious problems. For instance, suppose a bank account system has two processes: one for credits and another for debits. If these processes are not causally consistent, a customer might be able to deposit money into their account and withdraw it immediately, leading to a negative account balance.

In summary, causal consistency ensures that the order of operations in a distributed system is consistent with the causal order of the events in the real world.

instruction: The causal order is not a total order A total order allows any two elements to be compared, so if you have two elements, you can always say which one is greater and which one is smaller.
output:
However, in the causal order, this is not the case. You can't compare any two events directly, because one event may cause another and also be caused by it.

For example, consider two events: the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand and the outbreak of World War I. You can't say which one caused the other to happen, because the assassination caused the war, but the war also caused the assassination, in a sense.

Therefore, the causal order is not a total order.

instruction: The difference between a total order and a partial order is reflected in different data‐ base consistency models: Linearizability In a linearizable system, we have a total order of operations: if the system behaves as if there is only a single copy of the data, and every operation is atomic, this means that for any two operations we can always say which one happened first.
output:
ACID (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) is a popular consistency model for databases, and it supports linearizability. When a transaction is committed in an ACID system, it becomes visible to all clients and the database is left in a consistent state.

In a partial order system, on the other hand, we don't have a total order of operations. Instead, we have a set of rules for dealing with concurrent transactions. Each transaction is executed independently, and the system ensures that no transaction sees data that was written by another transaction before it committed.

Eventually Consistent is a popular consistency model for big data systems. It's a form of partial order consistency where the system ensures that all data will eventually be consistent, but not necessarily immediately.

instruction: Linearizability is not the only way of preserving causality—there are other ways too. A system can be causally consistent without incurring the performance hit of making it linearizable (in particular, the CAP theorem does not apply).
output:
One of the other ways to ensure causality is through the use of vector clocks. A vector clock assigns a vector of integers to each process, representing the number of steps taken by that process since the beginning of the computation.

For example, if we have two processes, P1 and P2, and P1 took 3 steps and P2 took 2 steps, then P1's clock would have values (3, 0) and P2's clock would have values (0, 2).

Vector clocks help ensure that when one process sends a message to another, the sending process records the receiving process's clock and the receiving process records the sending process's clock, creating a record of the interaction.

This record of interaction is called a vector timestamp. Vector timestamps are used to order messages that are concurrently sent, allowing a system to determine which message was sent first.

instruction: In fact, causal consistency is the strongest possible consistency model that does not slow down due to network delays, and remains available in the face of network failures [2, 42].

This is in contrast to the weaker eventual consistency model, which allows that the effects of concurrent events may be interleaved and observed out of order, even if the events themselves were not concurrent [42].

Causal consistency is often used in distributed systems where strong consistency is not necessary but real-time responsiveness is, such as in the context of large-scale web applications or online multiplayer games [42].

instruction: Capturing causal dependencies We won’t go into all the nitty-gritty details of how nonlinearizable systems can main‐ tain causal consistency here, but just briefly explore some of the key ideas.
output:
First, the system needs to have a global clock that assigns a timestamp to every event. Without a global clock, the system cannot determine the temporal order of events.

Second, the system needs a mechanism for propagating the effects of an event throughout the system. This is typically done using a technique called message passing, where each event triggers a message to be sent to other parts of the system.

Third, the system needs a way to ensure that messages are delivered in the correct order. This is often accomplished using a message queue or a distributed log, which maintains a sequence of all the messages that have been sent.

instruction: 342 | Chapter 9: Consistency and Consensus In order to maintain causality, you need to know which operation happened before which other operation. This is a partial order: concurrent operations may be pro‐ cessed in any order, but if one operation happened before another, then they must be processed in that order on every replica.
output:
To achieve this, a distributed system may use one of several synchronization algorithms. Two common ones are the Paxos and the Raft algorithms. Paxos is a consensus algorithm that elects a coordinator node. The coordinator node proposes a value, and all other nodes vote on that value. If a value receives enough votes, it becomes committed and is replicated across all nodes. This process is called consensus phase.

Raft is also a consensus algorithm, but it splits the process into three phases: Election, Log Replication, and Safety. In Election phase, a leader is elected, and the nodes follow that leader. In Log Replication phase, the leader sends the log entries to the followers, and the followers acknowledge each log entry. In Safety phase, the leader ensures that all log entries are replicated and the system is in a consistent state.

Both Paxos and Raft are complex algorithms and require careful consideration before implementation. However, once implemented, these algorithms ensure that distributed systems can maintain consistency and causality, even when facing network partitions.

instruction: The techniques for determining which operation happened before which other oper‐ ation are similar to what we discussed in “Detecting Concurrent Writes” on page 184. That section discussed causality in a leaderless datastore, where we need to detect concurrent writes to the same key in order to prevent lost updates.
output:
Here, we discuss causality in the context of a strongly consistent datastore. With a strongly consistent datastore, we can rely on the order of operations being exactly what the client specifies. However, there are still situations where we need to determine the order of operations, such as when multiple transactions contain conflicting writes.

To determine the order of operations, a datastore will often maintain a log of all operations, along with a timestamp for each operation. The log is used to provide an order in which the operations can be executed in the event of a conflict.

The datastore also assigns each transaction a unique identifier. This identifier can be used to determine the order of transactions, in the event that they contain conflicting writes.

When two transactions write to the same key, the datastore will check their transaction IDs to determine which transaction wrote first. The transaction with the lower ID is considered to have occurred before the transaction with the higher ID.

If two transactions have the same ID, the datastore will use the timestamps to determine the order. The transaction with the earlier timestamp is considered to have occurred before the transaction with the later timestamp.

instruction: To this end, the database keeps track of which data has been read by which transaction. Sequence Number Ordering Although causality is an important theoretical concept, actually keeping track of all causal dependencies can become impractical.
output:
To ensure transaction order, the database employs a technique called Sequence Number Ordering, also known as Two-Phase Locking.
In this technique, each transaction is given a unique number, called a sequence number, that it keeps track of internally.
Whenever a transaction wants to read data, it first checks if the last read sequence number of any existing transaction is greater than its own.
If it is, then the transaction waits until the database records that the other transaction has completed its current read operation.
This way, if multiple transactions try to read the same data at once, the one with the lower sequence number always gets the more up-to-date data.

instruction: Explicitly tracking all the data that has been read would mean a large overhead. However, there is a better way: we can use sequence numbers or timestamps to order events.
output:
We can store the sequence numbers or timestamps in the metadata of each event,
and use them to maintain order when reading data. This technique is called
event sourcing. Event sourcing has the following benefits:

* It makes the system more transparent. With event sourcing, we can see exactly
what happened at every point in time.
* It allows us to revert the system to any previous state.
* It simplifies distributed systems. In a distributed system, event sourcing
allows each microservice to maintain its own state and order, which reduces
the need for coordination and synchronization.

instruction: Ordering Guarantees | 343 Such sequence numbers or timestamps are compact (only a few bytes in size), and they provide a total order: that is, every operation has a unique sequence number, and you can always compare two sequence numbers to determine which is greater (i.e., which operation happened later).
output:
When you send a request to the server, the server generates a sequence number for the request and sends it back to you.

This sequence number can be used to ensure that your client always knows the order in which requests were processed by the server, even if there is a network delay.

If a client sends a request that has a sequence number less than or equal to the last sequence number it received from the server, the server will not process it and will instead return an error message.

This mechanism allows the client to resend requests if they were lost or if the client wants to reissue a request with a higher priority. It also allows the client to ensure that its view of the world is consistent with the server's, even in the face of network delays or other adverse conditions.

instruction: Noncausal sequence number generators If there is not a single leader (perhaps because you are using a multi-leader or leader‐ less database, or because the database is partitioned), it is less clear how to generate sequence numbers for operations.
output:
If you are using a multi-leader database, each leader can simply generate its own sequence number locally. However, if you want to ensure that all sequence numbers are monotonically increasing across the entire database, you will need some sort of synchronization mechanism. This can be implemented through a distributed locking protocol or a consensus protocol such as Paxos or Raft.

If you are using a leader-less database, such as a peer-to-peer database or a distributed hash table, things are even more complicated. Here, you will need to use a consensus protocol to ensure that all nodes agree on the next sequence number. This can be accomplished using a consensus protocol such as Paxos or Raft.

If the database is partitioned, the same logic applies: you will need some sort of synchronization mechanism to ensure that all sequence numbers are monotonically increasing across the entire database. This can be implemented using a distributed locking protocol or a consensus protocol.

instruction: Here, again, the sequence number is incon‐ sistent with causality. Lamport timestamps Although the three sequence number generators just described are inconsistent with causality, there is actually a simple method for generating sequence numbers that is consistent with causality.
output:
This method, called Lamport timestamps, uses a clock that is incremented
whenever a message is sent. Each process stores the current value of the clock
when it sends a message, and includes the value in the message. When the
message is received, the receiving process increments its clock and stores
the new value. The receiver then replies with its new clock value.

When two processes send messages to each other, they can therefore determine
which message arrived first by comparing their clocks. If process A's clock
value is greater than process B's clock value, then the message from A was
sent before the message from B.

When a process performs an action, it increments its own sequence number, and the receiving process records the sequence number of the sender. instruction: So far this description is essentially the same as the even/odd counters described in the last section. The key idea about Lamport timestamps, which makes them consis‐ tent with causality, is the following: every node and every client keeps track of the maximum counter value it has seen so far, and includes that maximum on every request.
output:
When a node or client receives a request with a timestamp higher than the one it currently has, it adopts the new value. If two requests arrive with the same timestamp, the one from the node is taken to be older: a node always considers its own requests to be older than those it issues.

This rule ensures that, if a node issues a request after receiving one from another node, it will include a timestamp larger than the one in the received request. In other words, a node always sends a timestamp that is greater than or equal to the timestamp of the most recent request it received.

This method of timestamping ensures that nodes always receive messages in the order they were sent, and it is the reason why Lamport timestamps are consistent with causality. This consistency property is crucial for ensuring that distributed systems can coordinate and maintain consistency.

instruction: Timestamp ordering is not sufficient Although Lamport timestamps define a total order of operations that is consistent with causality, they are not quite sufficient to solve many common problems in dis‐ tributed systems.
output:
For example, consider a distributed database with concurrent transactions T1 and T2. If T1 writes a value V1 to an object X, and T2 writes a value V2 to the same object X, but before T1 finishes writing V1, then T2's write operation will be assigned a later timestamp than T1's.
However, the value that T2 wrote should not replace T1's value, because T1 finished first.
Therefore, a timestamp-based consistency protocol is insufficient for maintaining consistency in distributed databases.

instruction: For example, consider a system that needs to ensure that a username uniquely identi‐ fies a user account. If two users concurrently try to create an account with the same username, one of the two should succeed and the other should fail.
output:
To solve this problem, the system could use a distributed lock. Each time a user tries to create a new account, the system requests a lock on the username. If the lock is granted, the system creates the account. If the lock request fails, the user must try again later.

The distributed locking mechanism could be implemented using a variety of techniques, such as a database lock or a leased lock. In either case, the lock ensures that only one user can create a new account with the given username at a time.

instruction: (We touched on this problem previously in “The leader and the lock” on page 301.) At first glance, it seems as though a total ordering of operations (e.g., using Lamport timestamps) should be sufficient to solve this problem: if two accounts with the same username are created, pick the one with the lower timestamp as the winner (the one who grabbed the username first), and let the one with the greater timestamp fail.
output:
However, it turns out that this simple solution is not sufficient: creating an account with a bogus timestamp (e.g., setting it to a date in the future) can let that account claim the name before the one with the correct timestamp.

To solve this problem, we use a technique called "time-stamped comparative ordering" (TSO), which combines ordering and comparison operations. With TSO, a system can determine that two operations are concurrent and not order them, even if they have different timestamps.

With TSO, the system keeps track of the sequence numbers of each operation, and the timestamp of the most recent operation that completed before the current one. When two operations arrive at roughly the same time, the system uses the sequence numbers and timestamps to determine their relative order.

For example, consider two operations, A and B, which both try to create an account with the same username. If A's sequence number is greater than B's sequence number, or A's timestamp is greater than B's timestamp, then A is considered to have occurred before B, and B fails.

instruction: To conclude: in order to implement something like a uniqueness constraint for user‐ names, it’s not sufficient to have a total ordering of operations—you also need to know when that order is finalized.
output:
One possible way to ensure that order is finalized is to have a single thread of execution
that handles all user‐name creation. This would ensure that the order of user‐name creation
is linear and, thus, finalized. Another way to ensure finality of order is to use a locking
mechanism to make all user‐name creation atomic. This would also ensure that the order of
user‐name creation is finalized.

However, the first approach, having a single thread of execution for user‐name creation,
has significant trade-offs. First, it would mean that all user‐name creation requests would
have to wait in line for that thread to become available. This could result in a backlog of
requests and a poor user experience for users who have to wait for their user names.

Another approach to ensure that order is finalized is to implement a queueing system where
user‐name creation requests are stored in a queue and then processed in the order they
were received. This would ensure that the order of user‐name creation is finalized while
allowing for multiple threads to create user names concurrently.

But if your program runs on a multiprocessor, things get much more complicated. In a multiprocessor system, multiple CPUs execute instructions simultaneously. This means that a program order on one CPU does not necessarily correspond to the program order on another CPU.

Each member maintains a log of the broadcast messages it has received, and when it receives a new message, it appends that message to its log. When a member receives a message, it checks the log of other members to ensure that the message is not too old. If a message is old, it is discarded. If the message is new, it is added to the member's log.

instruction: We can also turn it around, assume that we have linearizable storage, and show how to build total order broadcast from it. The easiest way is to assume you have a linearizable register that stores an integer and that has an atomic increment-and-get operation [28].
output:
To build a total order broadcast tree, follow these steps:
1. Initialize a root node, which is a linearizable register with initial value 0.
2. Each process Pi initializes a child node Ci of the root as a new linearizable register, sets its initial value to Pi's identifier, and sets its parent to be the root.
3. Each process Pj, having initialized a child Ci, sends the value of Ci to its parent, and waits for a reply from its parent containing the value of a sibling node.
4. When a process receives a reply from its parent with the value of a sibling, it adds the sibling's value to its own list of children.
5. After a process Pk has received replies from all its children, it sends its list of children to its parent.
6. When a process Pm receives a list of children from its parent, it merges the lists of its children and its own, and broadcasts the merged list to all its children.
7. A leaf process (a process with no children) sends its identifier to its parent.

The answer is: it's not easy. There are several reasons for this. First, the atomic increment-and-get operation required to create a linearizable integer must be implemented in hardware and must be globally visible. This means that every processor in the system must have a copy of this atomic operation, and the operation must be visible to all processors at all times.

Additionally, the atomic increment-and-get operation must be both atomic and conflict-free. Atomicity means that the operation must be completed as a single, indivisible unit. Conflict-freedom means that the operation cannot interfere with other operations.

Finally, the atomic increment-and-get operation must be implemented in such a way that it supports ordering guarantees. This means that the operation must be able to be ordered with respect to other operations in the system, and the order must be consistent across all processors.

instruction: As usual, if things never failed, it would be easy: you could just keep it in a variable on one node. The problem lies in handling the situation when network connections to that node are interrupted, and restoring the value when that node fails [59].

But what happens if that node is a critical one? You don't want to lose the information it stores.

An obvious solution is to replicate the data across multiple nodes [59]. But how do you ensure that the data on all nodes remains consistent?

The answer is through a distributed consensus algorithm.

This algorithm allows the nodes to reach agreement on a single value, even if some nodes fail.

One popular choice is the Raft algorithm [59], which ensures consistency by electing a leader node, maintaining a log, and replicating state changes.

When a node requests a state change, it first checks with the leader to ensure it's valid.

The leader then logs the change and replicates it to the followers [59]. Once a majority of the nodes have acknowledged the change, it becomes part of the consensus and is considered final.

instruction: That is, if you can solve one of these problems, you can transform it into a solution for the others. This is quite a profound and surprising insight! It is time to finally tackle the consensus problem head-on, which we will do in the rest of this chapter.
output:
In the consensus problem, each person in a group has a different opinion about the solution to a problem. The group's goal is to come to a consensus, or agreement, on the best solution.

The consensus problem is a classic problem in group decision making. It has been studied extensively in various fields, including psychology, sociology, and business management. Despite these efforts, however, the consensus problem remains an elusive goal.

There are several reasons why the consensus problem is so difficult to solve. One reason is that people's opinions are influenced by their own experiences and biases. Another reason is that people's opinions may be based on incomplete or inaccurate information. A third reason is that people may have conflicting goals or interests.

Despite these challenges, there are some strategies that can help groups reach a consensus. One strategy is to encourage open communication and active listening. Another strategy is to gather and share as much information as possible. A third strategy is to establish clear ground rules for the discussion and ensure that everyone follows them.

instruction: Distributed Transactions and Consensus Consensus is one of the most important and fundamental problems in distributed computing. On the surface, it seems simple: informally, the goal is simply to get sev‐ eral nodes to agree on something.
output:
But the problem is not as simple as it seems. If the nodes have identical copies of the same database, then consensus can be achieved through a simple majority vote, but when the databases are different, consensus becomes much more complex. In the distributed transaction context, consensus is needed to ensure the consistency of the distributed database.

A consensus algorithm can be seen as a method to reach agreement among distributed nodes on a single value, or on a single sequence of values. It can be viewed as a coordination protocol to ensure that all replicas of a distributed database agree on a single state.

instruction: If we want to maintain transaction atomicity (in the sense of ACID; see “Atomicity” on page 223), we have to get all nodes to agree on the outcome of the transaction: either they all abort/roll back (if anything goes wrong) or they all commit (if nothing goes wrong).
output:
Here's a simple two-phase commit protocol:

1. Prepare phase: Send a prepare request to all nodes involved in the transaction. Each node will respond with a vote: "yes, I can commit," or "no, I can't."
2. Commit phase: Once all nodes have voted "yes," the coordinator sends a commit request to all nodes, and they all commit the transaction. If any node votes "no," the coordinator aborts the transaction.

This protocol ensures that all nodes are aware of the outcome of the transaction before committing. It also allows for some degree of fault tolerance: if a node goes down during the prepare phase, the coordinator can retry sending the prepare request, and if it goes down during the commit phase, the coordinator can abort the transaction and retry later.

Why does the two-phase commit protocol ensure transaction atomicity?

instruction: This instance of consensus is known as the atomic commit problem.xii The Impossibility of Consensus You may have heard about the FLP result [68]—named after the authors Fischer, Lynch, and Paterson—which proves that there is no algorithm that is always able to reach consensus if there is a risk that a node may crash.
output:
The atomic commit problem is a particular challenge in distributed consensus protocols. It is the problem of ensuring consistency of a distributed transaction when some nodes may crash. In an atomic commit protocol, the transaction is considered to be completely successful or it is entirely abandoned. If a majority of nodes agree that the transaction is successful, it is committed. If a majority does not agree, it is aborted.

The atomic commit problem is crucial because it is a fundamental requirement for maintaining consistency in distributed systems. If transactions were allowed to be partially committed, then it would be impossible to maintain a consistent view of the data across all nodes.

Why does the atomic commit problem arise in distributed consensus protocols?

instruction: In a distributed system, we must assume that nodes may crash, so reliable consensus is impossible. Yet, here we are, discussing algorithms for achieving consensus. What is going on here?
output:
First, let me explain that consensus is not the same as agreement. Consensus algorithms ensure that all nodes agree on the same value, but agreement is possible without consensus.

Despite the challenges of achieving consensus in a distributed system, there are algorithms that can do it. The most famous are Paxos and Raft. Both Paxos and Raft achieve consensus by electing a leader and having all nodes follow its instructions.

However, consensus algorithms are not infallible. They are probabilistic, meaning that they will eventually reach consensus but may take a while or even fail in some cases.

So why do we care about consensus in a distributed system if it's not always reliable? Because in practice, consensus is reliable enough for most applications, and the cost of not having consensus can be high. For example, if multiple versions of the same data exist and are being concurrently updated, consensus ensures that all updates are consistent and no data is lost. In other words, consensus helps maintain data integrity and consistency, which is crucial in many distributed systems.
